proposals.title: Correlation-Enhanced Electron-Phonon Coupling: A Hubbard-Extended SSH Model for High-Pressure Hydride Superconductors
proposals.motivation: The discovery of high-Tc superconductivity in hydrogen-rich hydrides under extreme pressure has been primarily interpreted through strong electron-phonon coupling (EPC) within non-interacting electron frameworks like the Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) model. However, the role of electron-electron (e-e) correlations in these compressed, multi-component materials remains poorly understood, potentially explaining discrepancies between predicted and reported superconducting properties. This investigation is crucial for moving beyond the standard Migdal-Eliashberg theory to assess whether correlations suppress or cooperatively enhance the superconducting state in hydrides.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that on-site Hubbard-type electron-electron interactions, when incorporated into the SSH model, will significantly renormalize the effective electron-phonon coupling and phonon frequencies in high-pressure hydrides. This renormalization can either suppress Tc by reducing the density of states at the Fermi level or enhance it by mitigating Coulomb repulsion and promoting non-adiabatic effects.
proposals.method: We will develop a tight-binding model combining the SSH Hamiltonian for EPC with a Hubbard term for on-site e-e repulsion (U). The model will be solved using exact diagonalization for small clusters and Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) for larger systems to access the correlated electron regime. Key observables, including the effective EPC constant (λ_eff), superconducting gap, and charge susceptibility, will be computed as functions of U, pressure (modeled via hopping integrals), and temperature. The numerical code will be implemented using the TRIQS library for DMFT solvers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles-inspired, minimal model to quantify the interplay of correlations and EPC in high-pressure superconductors. It will deliver a phase diagram mapping superconducting stability against Hubbard U and pressure, offering a theoretical benchmark to interpret resistivity and magnetic susceptibility data from recent experiments. The results will clarify whether a purely strong-coupling EPC picture is sufficient or if correlation physics is essential for a complete understanding of hydride superconductivity.
proposals.novelty: While the SSH model is classic for EPC and the Hubbard model for correlations, their unified application to the specific context of high-pressure, high-Tc hydride superconductors is novel. This approach moves beyond the standard ab initio Migdal-Eliashberg treatments that typically neglect dynamic local correlations, addressing a key open question in the pre-2026 literature regarding the role of electronic interactions in these materials.

proposals.title: Long-range electron-phonon coupling in high-pressure hydrides: A determinant of Tc beyond conventional Migdal-Eliashberg theory
proposals.motivation: The record-high Tc reported in sulfur hydride and lanthanum hydride superconductors under high pressure challenges conventional Migdal-Eliashberg theory, which typically focuses on short-range interactions. The complex, extended structures of these hydride phases suggest that longer-range electron hopping and electron-phonon coupling may be critical, yet their role remains unexplored and could resolve discrepancies between predicted and observed superconducting properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that including longer-range electron hopping and non-local electron-phonon coupling in theoretical models of high-Tc hydrides will significantly enhance calculated Tc values and provide a more accurate microscopic explanation for their exceptional superconducting state.
proposals.method: We will develop an extended Hubbard-Holstein model incorporating second- and third-neighbor hopping (t', t'') and corresponding electron-phonon coupling terms. This model will be solved using Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) combined with continuous-time Quantum Monte Carlo (CT-QMC) impurity solvers to handle strong coupling and retardation effects non-perturbatively. The electronic structure inputs (hopping parameters, phonon spectra) will be derived from pre-2026 density functional theory (DFT) calculations on known hydride structures (e.g., H3S, LaH10). We will systematically vary the range of interactions to map their impact on the superconducting gap and Tc.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework for assessing the impact of long-range interactions in high-pressure hydride superconductors. It will deliver a revised theoretical upper bound for Tc in these materials and identify structural motifs that maximize beneficial non-local coupling, guiding the search for new high-Tc compounds.
proposals.novelty: While long-range Coulomb interactions are sometimes considered, a systematic investigation of long-range *electron-phonon* coupling in the context of real, complex hydride structures is novel and moves beyond the standard nearest-neighbor approximations dominating the pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Thermal Fluctuations and Topological Order: A Finite-Temperature Phase Diagram of the Su-Schrieffer-Heeger Model
proposals.motivation: The SSH model is a foundational paradigm for topological insulators and charge density wave (CDW) physics, yet its analysis is overwhelmingly confined to zero-temperature ground states. In real materials, finite temperature effects, including thermal fluctuations and entropy, are crucial for understanding phase stability and experimental observables. A comprehensive finite-temperature phase diagram is essential to bridge the gap between idealized topological classifications and the behavior of quasi-one-dimensional materials and polymers under realistic conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the topological and CDW phases of the SSH model are separated by distinct finite-temperature phase transitions, and that thermal fluctuations can induce a re-entrant phase or significantly shift the critical coupling strength compared to the ground-state prediction.
proposals.method: We will employ large-scale quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) simulations, specifically the stochastic series expansion (SSE) algorithm, to avoid the fermionic sign problem inherent in the SSH model. This will be complemented by analytical continuation via the Maximum Entropy method to extract spectral functions. We will compute the CDW order parameter, entanglement entropy, and relevant correlation functions as functions of temperature (T), electron-phonon coupling (λ), and phonon frequency (ω) to map the full T-λ-ω phase diagram.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first complete finite-temperature phase diagram for the SSH model, quantifying the thermal resilience of its topological edge states and CDW order. It will establish critical temperatures and crossover scales, offering concrete predictions for experiments on polyacetylene and engineered cold-atom simulators.
proposals.novelty: While the zero-temperature SSH model is textbook knowledge, its systematic finite-temperature analysis—particularly the interplay of thermal and quantum fluctuations in destroying or modifying topological order—remains largely unexplored and non-trivial, moving beyond the standard adiabatic approximation.

proposals.title: Quenched Disorder and the Resilience of the Peierls State in High-Pressure Hydride Superconductors
proposals.motivation: The recent surge in reports of high-temperature superconductivity in hydrides under extreme pressure has reignited interest in the underlying pairing mechanisms. Many candidate materials, like H3S and LaH10, are proposed to involve strong electron-phonon coupling and potential lattice instabilities, including Peierls-type dimerization. However, real samples contain intrinsic disorder and impurities from synthesis, whose effects on these delicate quantum states are poorly understood, potentially explaining discrepancies in reported superconducting properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that quenched disorder from non-stoichiometry or impurity atoms in high-pressure hydrides disrupts long-range Peierls order, leading to a fragmented, inhomogeneous ground state that can suppress or modify the superconducting transition temperature and magnetic screening response.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of theoretical modeling and analysis of published experimental data. The core theoretical framework will be a one-dimensional Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) model extended with Anderson-type disorder or random on-site potentials, solved via exact diagonalization and density matrix renormalization group (DMRG) methods to assess the stability of the dimerized phase. This will be complemented by mean-field analysis of a two-dimensional model relevant for layered hydride structures. We will correlate model predictions with systematic re-analysis of published resistance and magnetic susceptibility data from hydride superconductors, focusing on sample-to-sample variations linked to synthesis conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for understanding how intrinsic disorder influences competing orders in high-pressure hydrides. It will offer a critical lens to assess the robustness of claimed superconducting transitions and potentially explain why some groups fail to replicate high-Tc results. The findings will guide synthesis efforts towards more disorder-resilient materials.
proposals.novelty: While disorder studies are classic in condensed matter, applying this lens specifically to the nascent field of high-pressure hydride superconductors—where sample quality is a paramount concern—is novel. It directly connects the theorist's concept of a Peierls instability to the experimentalist's reality of 'dirty' samples under extreme conditions.

proposals.title: Doping-Induced Topological and Magnetic Phase Transitions in the Extended Su-Schrieffer-Heeger Model
proposals.motivation: The SSH model is a cornerstone for understanding topological phases and Peierls transitions in one-dimensional systems, but its canonical phase diagram is largely confined to the half-filled case. Real materials, including quasi-1D organic conductors and novel carbon-based nanostructures, inherently operate at various doping levels. A systematic mapping of the SSH model's phase diagram under doping is crucial to bridge this gap between idealized theory and experimental conditions relevant to correlated electron physics and potential topological materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that doping away from half-filling will destabilize the well-known topological and dimerized insulating phases, leading to novel metallic regimes, possible quantum critical points, and a complex interplay between topological edge states, magnetic order (from extended Hubbard terms), and charge density waves.
proposals.method: We will employ exact diagonalization and Density Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG) calculations to construct the ground-state phase diagram of the extended SSH model (including on-site U and nearest-neighbor V Hubbard interactions) as a function of chemical potential (doping) and electron-phonon coupling strength. Key observables will include charge and spin structure factors, entanglement entropy, local bond order, and topological invariants (e.g., Zak phase) to characterize phases. Finite-size scaling will be used to distinguish metallic, insulating, and ordered phases.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive phase diagram of the doped extended SSH model, directly applicable to interpreting transport and magnetic measurements in doped quasi-1D materials. It will clarify the robustness of topological edge states against doping and predict new correlated electron phenomena at non-integer fillings, offering theoretical guidance for material design.
proposals.novelty: While the half-filled SSH model is exhaustively studied, a systematic investigation of its doped phase diagram, particularly incorporating both electron-electron and electron-phonon interactions, remains largely unexplored and presents a non-trivial extension with significant implications for real materials.

proposals.title: General n-Component Gross-Neveu Theory and Its Application to the Low-Energy Physics of the SSH Model
proposals.motivation: The Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) model is a paradigmatic model for topological phases and polyacetylene, but its low-energy continuum description as a Gross-Neveu (GN) model is typically analyzed only for the specific cases of n=1 (real scalar field) and n=2 (complex scalar field) components. A systematic analysis for arbitrary n-component degeneracy is lacking. This generalization is crucial for understanding correlated electron systems with multiple orbital or band degeneracies under strong electron-phonon coupling, a relevant pre-2026 context for exploring mechanisms in complex materials, including high-pressure hydride superconductors where multi-band physics is prominent.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the critical behavior, symmetry breaking patterns, and topological excitations of the SSH model's low-energy sector exhibit a non-trivial dependence on the number of fermion components n, leading to distinct quantum phases not captured by the standard n=1,2 analyses.
proposals.method: We will derive the effective (1+1)-dimensional n-component Gross-Neveu action from the generalized SSH Hamiltonian via bosonization and renormalization group (RG) techniques. The analysis will employ large-n expansion methods to solve the gap equation and determine the phase diagram as a function of n and coupling strength. We will numerically corroborate the continuum field theory results using Density Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG) calculations on discrete SSH-like chains with engineered n-fold degeneracy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified theoretical framework for the SSH/Gross-Neveu universality class with arbitrary internal symmetry. It will directly contribute to the understanding of multi-band Peierls transitions and may offer new insights into the competition between pairing and charge density wave orders in quasi-one-dimensional correlated materials, including candidate high-temperature superconductors.
proposals.novelty: While the n=1,2 Gross-Neveu models are textbook examples, a comprehensive study of the physical consequences of varying n as a continuous parameter, connecting it directly to the lattice SSH model's degeneracy, represents a novel and unexplored direction in condensed matter field theory.

proposals.title: Conformal Field Theory of the Critical Su-Schrieffer-Heeger Model: Central Charge and Correlation Functions
proposals.motivation: The SSH model is a paradigmatic model for topological insulators and Peierls transitions in one-dimensional systems. While its topological and dimerized phases are well-studied, a precise conformal field theory (CFT) description of its critical point, separating dimerized and undimerized phases for spinless electrons, remains incomplete. Establishing this CFT is crucial for understanding the universal low-energy physics, such as entanglement scaling and critical exponents, which are relevant for quantum materials and cold-atom simulations.
proposals.hypothesis: The critical point of the spinless SSH model is described by a c=1 conformal field theory with specific compactification radius, and its correlation functions can be derived analytically from bosonization of the critical Dirac fermions.
proposals.method: We will employ bosonization techniques to map the critical SSH Hamiltonian to a free boson CFT. The central charge will be verified numerically using density matrix renormalization group (DMRG) calculations of the entanglement entropy scaling. Specific correlation functions, such as the single-particle Green's function and the dimer-dimer correlation function, will be computed within the CFT framework and compared to results from exact diagonalization and quantum Monte Carlo simulations for finite chains.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a complete analytical CFT characterization of the SSH critical point, yielding exact formulas for critical exponents and correlation functions. It will establish a foundational reference for interpreting numerical and experimental studies of one-dimensional topological criticality and serve as a benchmark for more complex interacting models.
proposals.novelty: While the SSH model is widely known, a dedicated study to fully derive its critical CFT data—central charge, operator product expansions, and finite-size scaling functions—from first principles and validate it with state-of-the-art numerical methods pre-2026 represents a clear gap in the literature.

proposals.title: Quantum Fluctuations and Lattice Instability in the 1D Su-Schrieffer-Heeger Model: An Abelian Bosonization Analysis
proposals.motivation: The Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) model is a cornerstone for understanding electron-phonon coupling and Peierls transitions in quasi-one-dimensional materials. However, its standard mean-field treatment neglects significant quantum fluctuations, which are crucial for describing low-temperature phases and potential superconducting correlations. An analytical, non-perturbative approach is needed to capture these quantum effects, especially relevant for interpreting the complex physics of high-pressure hydrides where strong coupling and low dimensionality may play a role.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying Abelian bosonization techniques from Luttinger liquid theory to the SSH model will yield an exact analytical description of its quantum-critical properties, revealing a renormalized phase diagram where quantum fluctuations suppress the Peierls gap and potentially stabilize a Luther-Emery liquid with power-law superconducting correlations.
proposals.method: We will map the fermionic SSH Hamiltonian onto a bosonic field theory using standard Abelian bosonization in 1D. The phonon field will be integrated out, leading to an effective sine-Gordon model for the electron-electron interactions mediated by the lattice distortion. The renormalization group (RG) flow equations for the coupling constants (Luttinger parameter K_rho and backscattering strength g) will be derived and solved analytically where possible, and numerically otherwise, to identify fixed points. The spectral functions and correlation functions (CDW, superconducting) will be calculated from the bosonized action.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first exact, bosonized solution for the quantum-critical SSH model, quantifying the suppression of the Peierls order by quantum fluctuations. It will establish a rigorous theoretical framework for analyzing strong electron-phonon coupling in 1D systems, offering new insights into the interplay between lattice instabilities and electronic correlations that may be pertinent to low-dimensional pathways in complex hydride superconductors.
proposals.novelty: While bosonization is standard for electronic interactions, its direct application to treat the quantum lattice dynamics of the SSH model non-perturbatively is novel. This approach moves beyond the adiabatic and mean-field approximations that dominate the pre-2026 literature on the model.

proposals.title: Ab Initio Prediction of Giant Spin Hall Conductivity in High-Pressure Superconducting Hydrides
proposals.motivation: The discovery of high-temperature superconductivity in hydrides under extreme pressure has opened a new frontier in condensed matter physics. Beyond their superconducting properties, these materials possess strong spin-orbit coupling and complex electronic structures that could generate substantial spin Hall effects, a key resource for spintronics. Quantifying this potential from first principles is crucial for identifying candidate materials for energy-efficient spin-current generation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific high-pressure hydride superconductors, due to their intrinsic heavy elements and predicted band topology, will exhibit giant spin Hall conductivities, orders of magnitude larger than conventional metals like Pt or β-Ta. This effect will be tunable with applied pressure and stoichiometry.
proposals.method: We will employ density functional theory (DFT) calculations using the Quantum ESPRESSO or VASP packages with fully relativistic pseudopotentials to include spin-orbit coupling (SOC). The electronic band structure and wavefunctions of candidate hydrides (e.g., H3S, LaH10, YHn) will be computed at various pressures. The intrinsic spin Hall conductivity (σ_SH) will be calculated using the Kubo formula linear response approach as implemented in the Wannier90 and WannierTools packages, constructing maximally localized Wannier functions for accurate Brillouin zone integration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, first-principles catalog of spin Hall angles for high-pressure hydrides, directly linking their superconducting phase space to spintronic functionality. It will establish theoretical guidelines for experimental searches for materials exhibiting concurrent superconductivity and large spin-charge conversion, a combination sought for superconducting spintronics.
proposals.novelty: While DFT is routinely used to predict superconducting Tc in hydrides, its application to calculate their spin Hall conductivity is novel. The proposal uniquely bridges the high-pressure superconductivity and computational spintronics communities by treating these exotic phases as potential spin-source materials, not just superconductors.

proposals.title: Probing the Thermal Spin Hall Effect in High-Temperature Superconductors and Hydrides Under Pressure
proposals.motivation: The spin Nernst effect, where a heat current generates a transverse spin current, is a fundamental thermospintronic phenomenon. Its investigation in correlated electron systems, particularly high-temperature superconductors and high-pressure hydrides, remains largely unexplored. Understanding this thermal spin Hall effect could reveal new spin-thermal coupling mechanisms and provide a novel, non-electrical probe for spin-orbit interactions in these complex materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a measurable thermal spin Hall conductivity exists in cuprate superconductors and in superconducting hydrides under pressure, and that its temperature and pressure dependence correlates with superconducting and magnetic phase transitions, offering a unique signature of spin-orbit coupling strength.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of first-principles density functional theory (DFT) calculations with spin-orbit coupling and Boltzmann transport theory within the relaxation-time approximation to compute the intrinsic spin Nernst conductivity. For candidate materials (e.g., YBa2Cu3O7-δ, LaH10), we will calculate the Berry curvature of Bloch states to evaluate the transverse spin response to a temperature gradient. The calculations will be performed as a function of temperature (incorporating electron-phonon scattering) and, for hydrides, as a function of applied pressure to map the evolution across superconducting phases.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first theoretical predictions for the thermal spin Hall effect in high-Tc cuprates and pressurized hydride superconductors. It will establish a theoretical framework for using thermal gradients to manipulate and probe spin states in these materials, potentially identifying new figures of merit for spintronic applications operating at elevated temperatures.
proposals.novelty: While the spin Nernst effect is studied in conventional metals and topological insulators, its systematic investigation in the context of high-temperature superconductivity and the high-pressure phase space of hydrides is novel. The proposal uniquely links Hirsch's expertise in superconductivity and high-pressure hydrides to the emerging field of spin caloritronics.

proposals.title: Competition between Charge and Spin Density Waves in Doped Hydride Superconductors: A Path to Enhanced Tc
proposals.motivation: The recent surge in high-pressure hydride superconductors has focused on phonon-mediated pairing, but the role of electronic correlations remains underexplored. The competition between charge density wave (CDW) and spin density wave (SDW) orders, central to cuprates and other correlated materials, could be crucial in hydrides, especially when doped away from half-filling where particle-hole symmetry is broken. Understanding this competition could reveal new mechanisms to stabilize or enhance superconductivity in these high-temperature systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in correlated hydride lattices, doping away from half-filling suppresses the SDW instability in favor of CDW fluctuations, and that this doped CDW regime provides a favorable electronic landscape for strengthening superconducting pairing and potentially raising the critical temperature (Tc).
proposals.method: We will employ dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT) combined with density functional theory (DFT+DMFT) to model representative hydride structures (e.g., H3S, LaH10) with explicit doping. The impurity solver will be a continuous-time quantum Monte Carlo (CT-QMC) algorithm. We will calculate the susceptibilities in the particle-hole channel to map the phase diagram of CDW and SDW instabilities as a function of doping and pressure, explicitly relaxing the common half-filling assumption. The evolution of the superconducting pairing vertex will be tracked concurrently.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic theoretical map of correlated electronic phases in doped high-pressure hydrides, directly linking doping-controlled CDW/SDW competition to superconducting properties. It will offer a new framework for interpreting resistivity and magnetic screening data in non-stoichiometric samples, potentially guiding the synthesis of hydrides with optimized doping for higher Tc.
proposals.novelty: While CDW/SDW competition is well-studied in cuprates, applying this paradigm to high-pressure hydrides—systems presumed to be conventional—and explicitly moving away from half-filling is novel. It connects Jorge Hirsch's recent critical analysis of hydride evidence directly to a concrete, correlation-based theoretical model for enhancement.

proposals.title: Non-Perturbative Corrections to the Mean-Field Phase Boundary in Strongly Correlated Superconducting Hydrides
proposals.motivation: The canonical mean-field BCS theory predicts a phase boundary at U=2V for the transition between superconducting and normal states. However, in the context of high-pressure hydride superconductors, where strong electron-phonon coupling and electronic correlations are prevalent, this prediction is known to deviate. A precise understanding of this boundary is critical for interpreting experimental data on critical temperatures and fields, which is currently complicated by potential confirmation bias in resistance measurements.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that non-perturbative corrections, arising from strong coupling and multi-band effects inherent to hydride systems, will qualitatively reshape the U=2V phase boundary, leading to a renormalized critical coupling ratio and altered thermodynamic scaling near the transition.
proposals.method: We will employ determinant Quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC) simulations on a Hubbard-Holstein model to treat electron-electron (U) and electron-phonon (V) interactions non-perturbatively. This will be complemented by Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) calculations for multi-band scenarios relevant to hydrides like H3S or LaH10. The phase boundary will be mapped by calculating the superconducting pairing susceptibility and charge compressibility across the (U, V, temperature) parameter space, with careful finite-size scaling in DQMC to mitigate sign problems where possible.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles, non-perturbative phase diagram for correlated superconductors, directly applicable to the high-pressure hydride family. It will establish quantitative benchmarks for when mean-field theory fails and offer a refined theoretical framework for analyzing reported Tc and Hc2 data, helping to distinguish genuine superconducting transitions from artifacts.
proposals.novelty: While the limitations of mean-field theory are known, a systematic non-perturbative calculation of the U-V phase boundary specifically tailored to the strong-coupling, multi-orbital physics of superconducting hydrides has not been performed. Our approach directly connects advanced computational many-body methods to the pressing interpretative challenges in the field.

proposals.title: Electron-Phonon Coupling and Magnetic Screening in High-Pressure Hydride Superconductors: A Combined Eliashberg and Spin-Fluctuation Analysis
proposals.motivation: The recent surge in reports of high-temperature superconductivity in hydrides under extreme pressure has been predominantly analyzed through either purely electronic (e.g., spin-fluctuation) or strong electron-phonon coupling frameworks. This dichotomy leaves open the critical question of how these interactions compete or cooperate to determine the superconducting transition temperature (Tc) and the crucial Meissner/screening response, a key signature your recent work has scrutinized. A unified approach is needed to resolve conflicting interpretations and identify the dominant pairing glue in these materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that including realistic electron-phonon coupling alongside repulsive electronic interactions within a multiband framework will significantly alter the predicted superconducting gap structure and critical temperature, and provide a more complete explanation for the magnetic screening properties reported in hydride superconductors.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined computational approach. First, density functional theory (DFT) calculations will be performed for candidate hydrides (e.g., H3S, LaH10) at high pressure to obtain electronic bands and phonon spectra. The electron-phonon coupling strength and Eliashberg spectral function α²F(ω) will be calculated using the EPW code. These results will then be integrated into a multiband Hubbard model with onsite repulsive interactions, treated within a random phase approximation (RPA) for spin fluctuations. The linearized gap equations will be solved self-consistently, mixing the attractive electron-phonon and repulsive spin-fluctuation pairing interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, first-principles-based framework to disentangle the contributions of phonons and electronic mechanisms to superconductivity in high-pressure hydrides. It will yield specific predictions for gap anisotropy and Tc, directly informing the interpretation of resistivity and magnetic susceptibility measurements. The results will establish whether a synergistic or antagonistic interaction between these channels exists, clarifying the fundamental pairing physics.
proposals.novelty: While electron-phonon calculations and purely electronic models for hydrides exist pre-2026, a self-consistent first-principles integration of both interactions to explicitly compute superconducting properties and link them to magnetic screening anomalies is novel. This moves beyond the prevailing 'either-or' analysis in the literature.

proposals.title: Thermally-Driven Crossover in Superconducting Phase Transitions: A Ginzburg-Landau Analysis for High-Pressure Hydrides
proposals.motivation: Theoretical studies of superconductors at zero temperature, particularly high-pressure hydrides, often reveal a crossover from a continuous (second-order) to a first-order phase transition as a function of parameters like impurity scattering or magnetic field. However, real-world observations and applications occur at finite temperature. It is crucial to understand if and how thermal fluctuations modify this crossover, as this directly impacts the thermodynamic stability, critical fields, and hysteretic behavior of these promising materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that finite temperature effects will significantly shift the parameter boundary for the continuous-to-first-order crossover compared to the zero-temperature case, and may even introduce a tricritical point in the temperature-parameter phase diagram for certain material regimes.
proposals.method: We will employ a generalized, finite-temperature Ginzburg-Landau (GL) theory, expanding the free energy to sixth order in the order parameter. The temperature dependence of the expansion coefficients (α, β, γ) will be derived from a microscopic Bardeen-Cooper-Schrieffer (BCS)-like model appropriate for strongly-coupled hydrides, incorporating effects of high pressure via density-of-states modifications. We will numerically solve the resulting GL equations to map the phase boundary between first- and second-order transitions across temperature and a control parameter (e.g., Ginzburg-Landau parameter κ or a pair-breaking strength). The analysis will focus on the behavior near the critical temperature Tc.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete theoretical framework for predicting the order of the superconducting transition in high-pressure hydrides at experimental conditions. It will clarify the role of thermal fluctuations in stabilizing or destabilizing the superconducting state against abrupt phase changes, offering a more accurate interpretation of magnetization and specific heat data reported in the literature.
proposals.novelty: While the zero-temperature crossover is known, a systematic finite-temperature GL analysis for high-pressure hydride superconductors, linking microscopic parameters to the transition order phase diagram, has not been performed. This bridges a gap between abstract quantum-critical models and experimental reality.

proposals.title: Precise Determination of the Tricritical Hubbard Interaction U for the Charge-Density-Wave to Spin-Density-Wave Transition
proposals.motivation: Understanding the competition between charge and spin order in strongly correlated electron systems is central to explaining phenomena like unconventional superconductivity and metal-insulator transitions. The Hubbard model serves as a key theoretical framework, but the precise nature of the transition between charge-density-wave (CDW) and spin-density-wave (SDW) phases, particularly the point where it changes character from continuous to first-order (the tricritical point), remains unresolved. A precise determination of this critical on-site interaction U is crucial for benchmarking advanced computational methods and for interpreting experimental results in materials like cuprates and organic conductors where such competing orders are observed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the CDW-SDW transition in the half-filled Hubbard model on a square lattice undergoes a clear tricritical point at a specific interaction strength U_t ≈ 3 (in units of hopping), where the transition changes from second-order to first-order, and that this point can be located with high precision using modern quantum Monte Carlo techniques.
proposals.method: We will employ large-scale determinant quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC) simulations, which are sign-problem-free at half-filling on bipartite lattices, to calculate the order parameters for CDW and SDW phases as a function of U. To precisely locate the tricritical point, we will perform finite-size scaling analysis on the Binder cumulants of the order parameters and compute the fourth-order energy cumulant to detect the latent heat signature of a first-order transition. Simulations will be conducted on lattice sizes up to 16x16, with careful control of temperature (down to T/t ~ 0.05) and Trotter error, using the ALF (Algorithms for Lattice Fermions) software library.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive numerical benchmark for the tricritical point in the 2D Hubbard model, resolving discrepancies in earlier literature. It will establish a more complete phase diagram for this canonical model, directly informing theoretical studies of competing orders in correlated materials. The methodology will also serve as a template for high-precision characterization of multicritical points in other quantum lattice models.
proposals.novelty: While the existence of a CDW-SDW transition is known, a precise, high-accuracy determination of the tricritical U using state-of-the-art finite-size scaling of Binder cumulants within DQMC has not been conclusively reported. This moves beyond qualitative phase diagrams to deliver a quantitative critical parameter.

proposals.title: Longer-Range Coulomb Interactions and the CDW-SDW Phase Boundary in High-Pressure Hydride Superconductors
proposals.motivation: The intense debate surrounding high-pressure hydride superconductors, such as H3S and LaH10, centers on whether observed zero-resistance states are genuine superconductivity or artifacts of measurement. A key theoretical question is the stability of competing charge density wave (CDW) and spin density wave (SDW) orders, which can suppress superconductivity. Current models often use simplified nearest-neighbor Hubbard-type interactions, neglecting the longer-range Coulomb repulsion expected in these dense, metallic hydrides, which may critically alter the phase diagram.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that including next-nearest-neighbor and longer-range Coulomb repulsion will significantly shift the CDW-SDW phase boundary, potentially stabilizing one ordered phase over the other and altering the predicted superconducting dome in the temperature-pressure phase diagram of candidate hydrides.
proposals.method: We will employ an extended Hubbard model on a relevant lattice (e.g., cubic or FCC) with on-site (U), nearest-neighbor (V1), and next-nearest-neighbor (V2) repulsive interactions. The model will be analyzed using mean-field theory and exact diagonalization for small clusters to map the ground-state phase diagram as a function of interaction ratios (V1/U, V2/U) and band filling. We will specifically compute the order parameters for CDW and SDW phases to determine their boundary. The computational framework will be implemented using Python/NumPy, with validation against known limits from the standard (V2=0) Hubbard model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more realistic theoretical baseline for assessing the stability of competing orders in high-pressure hydrides, informing the interpretation of resistivity and magnetic susceptibility data. It will quantify how longer-range interactions, inherent to these materials, modify the landscape for emergent superconductivity.
proposals.novelty: While the Hubbard model is standard, systematically incorporating longer-range Coulomb terms to study the CDW-SDW boundary in the context of high-pressure hydride superconductors is novel, moving beyond the oversimplified interactions typically used in prior analyses of these systems.

proposals.title: Analytical Characterization of the CDW-SDW Tricritical Point in Quasi-One-Dimensional Systems via Bosonization and Renormalization Group
proposals.motivation: The interplay between charge density wave (CDW) and spin density wave (SDW) orders is a fundamental problem in correlated electron systems, with direct relevance to high-temperature superconductivity in cuprates and organic conductors. Understanding the nature of their crossover, particularly at a putative tricritical point, is crucial for mapping the phase diagram of these materials. Pre-2026 numerical studies suggest such a point exists, but a precise analytical field-theoretic description is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the crossover between CDW and SDW phases in a quasi-one-dimensional system is governed by a tricritical point that can be fully characterized by the flow of scaling dimensions and coupling constants under a renormalization group (RG) analysis of an appropriate bosonized Hamiltonian.
proposals.method: We will employ the standard bosonization technique for a 1D electron gas with umklapp and backscattering interactions, mapping the fermionic Hamiltonian to a bosonic sine-Gordon model. The RG flow equations for the coupling constants (e.g., the Luttinger parameter K_ρ and spin gap Δ_s) will be derived and solved numerically. The tricritical point will be identified as the fixed point where the scaling dimensions of the CDW and SDW order parameters become equal and marginal, signaling the phase boundary termination.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a closed-form analytical framework for the CDW-SDW crossover, quantitatively predicting the location of the tricritical point in terms of microscopic parameters. It will offer a foundational field-theoretic explanation for numerical and experimental observations in correlated materials, potentially informing the search for novel ordered states near quantum critical points.
proposals.novelty: While bosonization and RG are established, their combined application to explicitly solve for and characterize the *tricritical* point separating CDW and SDW phases—rather than just the transitions—is novel. This moves beyond phase diagram mapping to a fundamental analytical understanding of the multicriticality.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Electronic Pairing Glue in High-Pressure Hydrides: A Critical Test of Phonon-Mediated Mechanisms
proposals.motivation: The discovery of high-temperature superconductivity in hydrides under extreme pressure has been predominantly interpreted within a conventional phonon-mediated BCS/strong-coupling framework. However, the exceptionally high critical temperatures and complex electronic structures of these materials suggest that purely electronic mechanisms, such as spin or charge fluctuations, could provide significant or even dominant contributions to the pairing glue. Resolving this is crucial for understanding the fundamental limits of superconductivity in these systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in key high-pressure hydride superconductors (e.g., H3S, LaH10), the dominant pairing interaction is not phononic but arises from electronic correlations, and that this can be quantified by distinct signatures in their magnetic and transport responses under pressure.
proposals.method: We will perform first-principles density functional theory (DFT) calculations coupled with dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT) to compute the full frequency-dependent electron self-energy, isolating the electronic contribution. This will be complemented by calculating the magnetic susceptibility and spin-fluctuation spectra using the random phase approximation (RPA). We will then implement a multi-orbital Eliashberg theory framework, explicitly separating the electron-phonon spectral function α²F(ω) from the electron-boson coupling function derived from spin fluctuations, to compute Tc and compare it with experimental values. Pressure-dependent trends in calculated magnetic field penetration depth and NMR relaxation rates will provide testable predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, first-principles-based metric to assess the relative strength of electronic versus phononic pairing in hydride superconductors. It will establish a more rigorous theoretical framework for interpreting high-pressure experiments and guide the search for new materials by identifying descriptors for electronic glue.
proposals.novelty: While electronic mechanisms are often invoked for cuprates, applying this rigorous quantitative separation of pairing components to the high-pressure hydrides—materials widely assumed to be phonon-driven—is a significant and non-obvious departure. It directly tests the core assumption of conventionality in this new class of high-Tc superconductors.

proposals.title: DMFT Phase Diagram of the Bond-Charge Repulsion Model: Implications for Correlated Hydrides and Pseudogap Physics
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the bond-charge (or extended Hubbard) Hamiltonian is a cornerstone model for correlated electron phenomena, including charge density waves, superconductivity, and the pseudogap phase in cuprates. Its phase diagram in the strongly correlated regime, particularly under the influence of bond-charge repulsion, remains poorly mapped due to the limitations of static mean-field and perturbative methods. This gap hinders the interpretation of anomalous transport and magnetic properties in complex materials, including high-pressure hydride superconductors where electronic correlations and lattice effects intertwine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) to the bond-charge repulsion model will reveal a rich phase diagram featuring competing orders, including a robust pseudogap-like phase and a dome of unconventional superconductivity, driven by non-local correlation effects beyond the standard Hubbard model.
proposals.method: We will implement a single-site DMFT loop for the bond-charge repulsion Hamiltonian on a square lattice, using a continuous-time quantum Monte Carlo (CT-QMC) impurity solver (specifically the hybridization expansion algorithm) to handle strong local interactions. The non-local bond-charge term will be incorporated self-consistently via the DMFT self-energy and the lattice Green's function. We will compute the temperature-dependent phase diagram as a function of on-site Coulomb repulsion (U) and bond-charge repulsion (V), identifying phases through order parameters and spectral functions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first numerically exact, finite-temperature phase diagram for the strongly correlated bond-charge model, directly linking model parameters to emergent phases. It will offer a new theoretical framework for analyzing the interplay of correlation and non-local interactions in candidate superconducting hydrides under pressure and in cuprate-like materials, potentially explaining anomalous resistivity and magnetic screening observations.
proposals.novelty: While DMFT is standard for the Hubbard model, its rigorous application to the bond-charge repulsion Hamiltonian to map finite-temperature phase competition is novel. This approach moves beyond static mean-field treatments prevalent in pre-2026 literature, capturing dynamical correlation effects crucial for pseudogap and superconducting physics.

proposals.title: Quantum Simulation of Bond-Charge Interactions in Optical Lattices: A Platform for Validating Models of High-Temperature Superconductivity
proposals.motivation: The microscopic origin of high-temperature superconductivity in hydrides and cuprates remains intensely debated, with bond-charge (or correlated hopping) models proposed as a key mechanism. However, testing these models in real materials is confounded by chemical complexity, pressure, and competing orders. A clean, tunable quantum simulator is needed to isolate and validate the role of occupation-dependent tunneling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an engineered occupation-dependent tunneling (bond-charge) term in an ultracold fermionic quantum gas will lead to measurable signatures of enhanced pairing correlations and d-wave symmetry, providing direct evidence for its sufficiency in driving high-Tc-like superconductivity.
proposals.method: We will implement a Fermi-Hubbard model with occupation-modulated tunneling using ultracold 6Li in a state-dependent optical lattice, where the tunneling amplitude is conditioned on the occupation of a neighboring site via laser-induced Raman coupling. This engineered bond-charge term will be calibrated via quantum gas microscopy. The system will be quenched from a high-temperature state, and pairing correlations, spectral functions, and transport will be measured via Bragg spectroscopy, time-of-flight, and single-site imaging to map the phase diagram as a function of interaction and bond-charge strength.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct experimental realization and benchmark of a pure bond-charge interaction in a quantum many-body system. It will establish cold atoms as a validation platform for theoretical models of unconventional superconductivity, informing the search for higher-Tc materials by identifying the essential ingredients for robust pairing.
proposals.novelty: While cold-atom simulations of the Fermi-Hubbard model exist, the explicit engineering and isolation of the occupation-dependent tunneling term—a central but untested component of several high-Tc theories—is a novel quantum resource that moves beyond simple on-site interaction simulations.

proposals.title: Competition and Cooperation of Phonon and Paramagnon Mediated Pairing in High-Pressure Hydride Superconductors
proposals.motivation: The recent surge in reports of near-room-temperature superconductivity in high-pressure hydrides (e.g., H3S, LaH10) has reignited debate over the pairing mechanism. While strong electron-phonon coupling is often invoked, the proximity to magnetic instabilities and the role of spin fluctuations (paramagnons) remain unclear, especially given the complex interplay of high pressure and temperature. Determining the dominant pairing glue is critical for validating theoretical models and guiding the search for new high-Tc materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in high-pressure hydride superconductors, the superconducting state arises from a complex interplay where paramagnon-mediated repulsion in specific channels competes with and potentially enhances the phonon-mediated attraction, leading to an effective pairing interaction distinct from pure BCS or spin-fluctuation scenarios.
proposals.method: We will employ first-principles density functional theory (DFT) calculations to determine the electronic structure and phonon spectra of candidate hydrides (e.g., YHn, CaH6) under pressure. We will then compute the full anisotropic Eliashberg function, incorporating both electron-phonon coupling and spin-fluctuation contributions derived from the random phase approximation (RPA) for the spin susceptibility. The coupling strengths and the resulting superconducting gap symmetry and Tc will be solved self-consistently using the Migdal-Eliashberg formalism, explicitly treating the competition between the two interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, material-specific framework to disentangle phononic and paramagnonic contributions to pairing in hydrides. It will clarify whether reported high Tcs can be explained by phonons alone or require a spin-fluctuation component, directly addressing the ongoing debate on the mechanism. The results will establish a predictive methodology for assessing the pairing character in other high-pressure superconductors.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have often considered these mechanisms in isolation, our proposal integrates them explicitly within a first-principles Eliashberg framework for hydrides, directly testing for cooperative or competitive effects. This moves beyond the simplistic 'which one' question to a detailed 'how much of each' analysis.

proposals.title: Paramagnon-Mediated d-wave Superconductivity in Realistic Cuprate Fermi Surfaces: A Quantitative Study
proposals.motivation: The paramagnon-mediated pairing mechanism, successfully applied to explain superconductivity in cubic systems like Sr2RuO4 and certain hydrides under pressure, remains largely untested on the realistic, anisotropic Fermi surfaces of cuprate superconductors. A quantitative calculation bridging this established theoretical framework with the specific electronic structure of cuprates is crucial to assess its viability as a universal mechanism for high-Tc d-wave pairing, moving beyond idealized band models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the paramagnon exchange mechanism, when calculated on ab initio-derived cuprate Fermi surfaces, will yield a robust d-wave pairing instability with a transition temperature scale consistent with experimental observations, and that the detailed momentum structure of the gap will be sensitive to Fermi surface topology and the location of the van Hove singularity.
proposals.method: We will employ the random phase approximation (RPA) to calculate the spin-fluctuation (paramagnon) susceptibility for realistic two-dimensional Fermi surfaces obtained from tight-binding fits to ARPES data or DFT calculations for prototypical cuprates like Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ. The effective pairing interaction will be derived from this susceptibility and used to solve the linearized Eliashberg equation on a fine momentum grid. The computational core will involve iterative matrix diagonalization of the gap equation, implemented in a custom Fortran/Python code, to extract the leading eigenvalue (pairing strength) and eigenfunction (gap symmetry and structure).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, material-specific quantification of the paramagnon pairing strength in canonical cuprates, establishing a clear benchmark for the mechanism. It will predict detailed gap anisotropy and its doping dependence, offering testable signatures for spectroscopic experiments. The results will either strongly support paramagnon exchange as a primary mechanism or reveal its quantitative shortcomings, guiding the search for additional ingredients.
proposals.novelty: While paramagnon models exist, applying the full RPA-based pairing formalism to *realistic*, material-specific cuprate band structures—rather than simple cubic or nearest-neighbor tight-binding models—is a novel and necessary step for a definitive quantitative assessment. This bridges a critical gap between abstract mechanism and material reality.

proposals.title: Quantitative d-wave Tc from paramagnon-mediated pairing: A computational study of high-pressure hydrides
proposals.motivation: The field of high-pressure hydride superconductivity is dominated by reports of conventional s-wave pairing, but the role of spin fluctuations and unconventional pairing remains underexplored. Given the complex magnetic and structural phase diagrams of these materials, a quantitative assessment of d-wave pairing driven by paramagnon exchange is crucial to understand the full spectrum of possible superconducting states and to guide the search for higher-Tc mechanisms beyond electron-phonon coupling.
proposals.hypothesis: Paramagnon exchange in high-pressure hydrides can provide a pairing glue strong enough to yield a d-wave superconducting state with a transition temperature (Tc) that is quantitatively significant and potentially competitive with phonon-driven Tc in certain regions of the phase diagram.
proposals.method: We will solve the linearized Eliashberg equations on the imaginary frequency axis for a d-wave order parameter, explicitly incorporating a dynamic paramagnon exchange interaction derived from first-principles random phase approximation (RPA) calculations. The electronic structure will be obtained via density functional theory (DFT) for specific candidate hydrides (e.g., LaH10, YHn) under pressure. The numerical solution will employ a momentum-resolved Nambu-Gor'kov formalism, discretizing the Fermi surface and solving the resulting eigenvalue problem for the leading pairing instability using iterative diagonalization techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first explicit, material-specific Tc estimates for d-wave pairing in high-pressure hydrides, moving beyond qualitative arguments. It will establish whether paramagnon mechanisms are negligible or a viable alternative pathway to high-Tc in these systems, thereby refining the theoretical framework for interpreting resistance and magnetic screening data.
proposals.novelty: While paramagnon-mediated d-wave pairing is well-studied in cuprates, its quantitative application to the high-pressure hydride superconductors, typically assumed to be purely conventional, is novel. The explicit computation of Tc from first-principles spin-fluctuation spectra for these materials represents a significant advance over phenomenological models.

proposals.title: Non-perturbative Validation of Paramagnon-Mediated d-wave Superconductivity via Determinant Quantum Monte Carlo
proposals.motivation: The paramagnon-mediated pairing mechanism is a leading candidate for explaining unconventional superconductivity, including in high-temperature cuprates. However, theoretical predictions from perturbative methods like the Random Phase Approximation (RPA) require non-perturbative validation to assess their robustness beyond the weak-coupling regime. This is crucial for establishing a reliable theoretical foundation for high-Tc superconductivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that determinant quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC) simulations of a two-dimensional Hubbard model with intermediate coupling will confirm the existence of a d-wave superconducting phase driven by paramagnon fluctuations, as predicted by RPA, but will also reveal non-perturbative corrections to the transition temperature and pairing strength.
proposals.method: We will employ the finite-temperature determinant quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC) algorithm, implementing the Blankenbecler-Scalapino-Sugar formalism, to simulate the single-band Hubbard model on a square lattice. The method will utilize a Trotter decomposition and Hirsch-Fye impurity solver, with careful management of the fermion sign problem via constrained path or phase-free approximations as needed. We will compute the superconducting pairing susceptibility and spin susceptibility to directly extract the effective pairing interaction and compare it to RPA predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, non-perturbative benchmark for the paramagnon pairing scenario, quantifying the accuracy and limitations of RPA. It will deliver a quantitative phase diagram linking spin fluctuations to d-wave pairing strength, offering critical insights for materials design. The results will either solidify or challenge a foundational theoretical framework in the field of unconventional superconductivity.
proposals.novelty: While DQMC has been used to study the Hubbard model, its direct application to validate the specific pairing vertex and transition temperature predictions of the RPA paramagnon theory for d-wave superconductivity represents a novel and crucial test. This bridges a key gap between perturbative many-body theory and exact numerical simulation.

proposals.title: Functional Renormalization Group Analysis of Competing SDW and d-wave Superconductivity in Iron-Pnictides and Cuprates
proposals.motivation: Understanding the interplay between spin-density-wave (SDW) magnetism and unconventional superconductivity in correlated electron systems remains a central challenge. Pre-2026 studies of iron-based and cuprate superconductors often treat these orders within biased approximations like RPA, which can artificially favor one instability over another. A more unbiased theoretical framework is needed to elucidate the true nature of their competition and potential coexistence.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying the functional renormalization group (fRG) to a unified low-energy model will reveal that the leading instability is highly sensitive to microscopic parameters and that a near-degeneracy between SDW and d-wave superconducting channels underpins the complex phase diagrams of these materials.
proposals.method: We will implement a temperature-flow fRG scheme for a two-dimensional Hubbard-type model with appropriate tight-binding parameters for representative pnictide and cuprate systems. The method will treat all one-particle irreducible vertices (4-point, 6-point) on equal footing, integrating out high-energy modes to track the flow of effective interactions. We will specifically code the flow equations for the SDW and d-wave pairing channels, using a patching scheme for the momentum dependence of the interactions to identify the leading divergence in the vertex function.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, unbiased theoretical phase diagram showing the evolution from SDW to d-wave superconductivity as a function of doping and interaction strength. It will clarify whether these orders are truly exclusive or can microscopically coexist, offering a fundamental explanation for the proximity of magnetic and superconducting phases in experimental phase diagrams.
proposals.novelty: While fRG has been applied to superconductivity, its direct application to treat SDW and d-wave pairing with full momentum resolution and without a priori RPA-like channel selection is novel for the specific context of high-temperature superconducting materials, directly addressing the bias inherent in many previous comparative studies.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Spin-Fluctuation Mediated Pairing Interaction in High-Pressure Hydrides via Inelastic Neutron Scattering
proposals.motivation: The microscopic pairing mechanism in high-pressure hydride superconductors remains intensely debated, with spin-fluctuation-mediated pairing being a leading candidate. However, theoretical calculations of the pairing strength and Tc rely heavily on approximations for the dynamic spin susceptibility, χ(q,ω), which have not been directly validated against experiment. A direct, quantitative comparison is essential to move beyond phenomenological models and confirm or refute the spin-fluctuation theory in these materials.
proposals.hypothesis: The experimentally measured dynamic spin susceptibility from inelastic neutron scattering (INS), when used as direct input for pairing calculations via the linearized Eliashberg equations, will yield a superconducting transition temperature (Tc) and gap symmetry consistent with reported transport and magnetic measurements in a benchmark hydride like H3S or LaH10.
proposals.method: We will perform high-pressure inelastic neutron scattering (INS) measurements on a candidate hydride (e.g., H3S) using a Paris-Edinburgh cell at a spallation source (e.g., SNS or ISIS) to obtain the full momentum (q) and energy (ω) dependent spin fluctuation spectrum, S(q,ω). This measured S(q,ω) will be directly converted to the imaginary part of the spin susceptibility, χ''(q,ω). This experimental χ''(q,ω) will serve as the sole input for the electron-boson coupling function in numerical solutions of the anisotropic, multiband Eliashberg equations, performed using established codes (e.g., based on the EPW package). The calculated Tc and gap structure will be compared to experimental values.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct experimental constraint on the spin-fluctuation pairing interaction in hydride superconductors, moving the field from indirect inference to quantitative validation. It will establish a rigorous benchmark for ab initio calculations of spin fluctuations and pairing in strongly coupled, high-pressure systems. A successful match would strongly corroborate the spin-fluctuation mechanism, while a discrepancy would necessitate a fundamental re-evaluation of the pairing glue.
proposals.novelty: While INS has been used to study spin fluctuations in cuprates and iron-based superconductors, its direct application as quantitative input for first-principles pairing calculations in high-pressure hydrides is unprecedented. This closes the loop between a key experimental observable and a fundamental theoretical prediction of superconductivity.

proposals.title: Inverting the Gap Equation: Reconstructing the Spin-Fluctuation Spectrum from a Measured d-Wave Order Parameter in High-Pressure Hydrides
proposals.motivation: The recent surge in reports of high-temperature superconductivity in hydrides under extreme pressure has reignited debate over the pairing mechanism. While d-wave symmetry is often a hallmark of spin-fluctuation-mediated pairing, directly confirming this origin in new materials is challenging. An inverse approach, deducing the effective interaction from the observed gap, would provide a crucial, model-agnostic test for spin-fluctuation scenarios in these systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The momentum-dependent d-wave superconducting gap function, measured via techniques like ARPES or penetration depth anisotropy, contains sufficient information to uniquely reconstruct the dominant structure of the underlying spin-fluctuation spectrum through an inversion of the linearized gap equation.
proposals.method: We will employ a constrained optimization framework to invert the linearized Eliashberg equation, Δ_k ∝ ∫ dq V_{k-q} Δ_q / E_q. Starting from a high-fidelity experimental d-wave gap map Δ_k(θ) for a candidate material, we will parameterize the effective pairing interaction V(q) and use a gradient-descent algorithm to minimize the difference between the input Δ_k and the gap computed from V(q). Regularization terms will be included to ensure physical smoothness and causality of the inferred spectrum. The inversion will be performed on simulated data from known spin-fluctuation models to validate the method before application to real experimental datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct computational tool to test spin-fluctuation pairing hypotheses in unconventional superconductors without assuming a specific microscopic model. It will yield a quantitative, experimentally derived effective interaction spectrum for high-pressure hydrides, offering a new pathway to discriminate between competing pairing mechanisms and potentially guiding ab initio calculations.
proposals.novelty: While forward calculations from interactions to gaps are standard, the explicit inversion of the gap equation to recover a detailed V(q) from a real material's gap symmetry is a non-trivial and underexplored inverse problem, moving beyond qualitative symmetry comparisons to quantitative interaction spectroscopy.

proposals.title: Fate of Van Hove Singularity Enhancement in Strongly-Coupled Superconductors: A Non-Perturbative Study of High-Pressure Hydrides
proposals.motivation: The discovery of high-Tc superconductivity in hydrides like H3S and LaH10 has been attributed to conventional electron-phonon coupling, with Van Hove singularities (VHS) near the Fermi level proposed as a key mechanism for Tc enhancement within weak-coupling BCS theory. However, the coupling strength in these materials is estimated to be large (λ ~ 2), placing them in the strong-coupling regime where standard perturbative Migdal-Eliashberg theory and VHS analyses may break down. It is crucial to determine if the VHS enhancement mechanism persists or transforms fundamentally when non-adiabatic and retardation effects become significant.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in the strong-coupling regime relevant to high-Tc hydrides, the enhancement of superconductivity due to a Van Hove singularity is qualitatively transformed, potentially leading to a saturation or even a suppression of Tc compared to weak-coupling predictions, due to the breakdown of the Migdal approximation and strong renormalization of the electronic spectrum.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of advanced computational many-body techniques. First, we will use Density Functional Theory (DFT) to obtain electronic structures and phonon spectra for model systems and real hydrides (e.g., H3S). We will then solve the fully momentum-dependent, non-adiabatic Eliashberg equations using the EPW code, explicitly incorporating the VHS. To go beyond the Migdal approximation, we will implement and apply the Diagrammatic Monte Carlo (DiagMC) method for the Holstein and Fröhlich models with a VHS density of states, allowing for numerically exact treatment of the electron-phonon vertex at strong coupling. This will be complemented by Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) to assess local correlation effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, non-perturbative map of how Van Hove physics interacts with strong electron-phonon coupling. It will establish theoretical limits for Tc enhancement from singular densities of states and deliver concrete, material-specific predictions for the maximum achievable Tc in hydride systems under pressure, moving the field beyond extrapolations from weak-coupling theory.
proposals.novelty: While VHS effects in weak coupling and strong-coupling Eliashberg theory for constant density of states are known, a non-perturbative, diagrammatically controlled investigation of their interplay in the relevant parameter space for modern high-pressure superconductors has not been performed. The application of DiagMC to this realistic materials problem is a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Spin-Fluctuation-Mediated Superconductivity from Singular Density of States in High-Pressure Hydrides
proposals.motivation: The prevailing electron-phonon paradigm struggles to fully explain the high critical temperatures reported in hydride superconductors under pressure. Recent theoretical work suggests these materials may host singular or highly peaked electronic densities of states (DOS) near the Fermi level due to band structure effects under strain. This creates a novel platform to explore non-phononic pairing, which is a central unresolved question in condensed matter physics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in hydrides under high pressure, a singular or near-singular DOS dramatically enhances electronic correlation effects, enabling a spin-fluctuation-mediated pairing mechanism that can support high-temperature superconductivity without strong electron-phonon coupling.
proposals.method: We will employ first-principles density functional theory (DFT) calculations to map the electronic structure and identify pressure regimes leading to van Hove singularities or flat bands in candidate hydrides (e.g., H3S, LaH10). Using the random phase approximation (RPA) and functional renormalization group (fRG) methods, we will calculate the effective electron-electron interaction and the leading pairing instability. This will be contrasted with standard Eliashberg theory calculations of the electron-phonon coupling strength to isolate the electronic mechanism.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete theoretical framework to assess the role of non-phononic mechanisms in high-Tc hydrides, moving beyond the confirmation bias of attributing all superconductivity to phonons. It will establish quantitative criteria—linking DOS features, spin susceptibility, and Tc—to identify materials where electronic pairing dominates.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in directly connecting the singular DOS, a specific consequence of high-pressure crystal structure, to a quantifiable spin-fluctuation pairing channel in hydrides, rather than treating it as a generic enhancement factor for phonon-mediated coupling.

proposals.title: Critical Scattering and the Destruction of Van Hove-Enhanced Superconductivity in High-Pressure Hydrides
proposals.motivation: The reported high-Tc superconductivity in hydrides like H3S and LaH10 is often attributed to strong electron-phonon coupling enhanced by Van Hove singularities (VHS) in the electronic density of states near the Fermi level. However, the role of disorder and quasiparticle scattering, inherent in high-pressure synthesized samples, remains poorly quantified. Determining the scattering rate at which VHS-driven Tc enhancement is suppressed is crucial for distinguishing intrinsic superconducting mechanisms from extrinsic sample quality effects, directly addressing concerns of confirmation bias in the field.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that there exists a critical, material-dependent quasiparticle scattering rate Γ_c, above which the Tc enhancement from a Van Hove singularity is completely negated, rendering the system a conventional, low-Tc superconductor.
proposals.method: We will employ numerical solutions of the full-bandwidth, anisotropic Eliashberg equations, incorporating a finite quasiparticle lifetime (Γ) via a self-energy iΓ in the electron Green's function. The electronic structure near the VHS will be modeled using tight-binding parameters derived from published DFT calculations for H3S and LaH10. We will systematically vary Γ and the electron-phonon coupling strength λ to compute the suppression of Tc, identifying Γ_c as the point where Tc returns to its non-VHS baseline. The code will be implemented using an iterative numerical solver on a momentum-space grid, benchmarking against known clean-limit results.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative theoretical framework to assess the robustness of VHS-enhanced superconductivity against disorder, offering a concrete metric (Γ_c) for evaluating the intrinsic potential of candidate hydride materials. It will establish clear guidelines for the sample quality required to observe the predicted high Tc, thereby informing and refining high-pressure experimental synthesis strategies.
proposals.novelty: While the Eliashberg theory of clean systems with VHS is established, a systematic study of the *critical* scattering rate that destroys the enhancement is novel. This directly connects a fundamental theoretical limit to the practical, sample-dependent challenges prevalent in high-pressure hydride research.

proposals.title: Enhancement of Superconductivity and Correlation-Driven Phenomena Near a Van Hove Singularity: A DMFT Study for High-Pressure Hydrides
proposals.motivation: Recent reports of high-temperature superconductivity in hydrides under extreme pressure, such as H3S and LaH10, are often linked to electronic structures featuring Van Hove singularities (VHS) in their density of states (DOS). However, standard DFT methods fail to capture the strong local electron correlation effects and quasiparticle renormalization near these singularities, which are crucial for accurately predicting superconducting Tc and understanding competing magnetic orders. A proper theoretical framework is needed to resolve debates on the fundamental pairing mechanisms in these materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the application of Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) to models with a singular DOS will reveal a significant enhancement of both superconducting pairing and local magnetic moments near the VHS, driven by self-consistent quasiparticle mass renormalization and correlation effects not captured by weak-coupling approaches.
proposals.method: We will implement a DMFT loop on a two-dimensional tight-binding lattice model engineered to host a VHS at the Fermi level. The impurity solver will be a continuous-time quantum Monte Carlo (CT-QMC) algorithm in the hybridization expansion, chosen for its accuracy at intermediate coupling and finite temperatures. The self-consistent solution will yield the local Green's function, self-energy, and spectral function, allowing us to compute the renormalized quasiparticle mass, spin susceptibility, and pairing vertex. We will specifically scan the doping level relative to the VHS and the Hubbard U parameter to map the phase diagram.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, first-principles-based framework for assessing the role of correlations in VHS systems, directly applicable to high-pressure hydride superconductors. It will yield concrete predictions for the correlation-enhanced density of states and its impact on Tc, helping to interpret experimental resistivity and magnetic screening data. The results will establish whether strong correlations near a VHS suppress or enhance superconductivity relative to Migdal-Eliashberg theory.
proposals.novelty: While DMFT has been applied to Hubbard models, its systematic application to specifically model and disentangle the effects of a singular DOS from generic band effects in the context of realistic superconducting hydrides is novel. This approach moves beyond the standard practice of using the bare DFT DOS in Eliashberg equations, self-consistently treating the feedback between correlations and the electronic structure.

proposals.title: Generalized Discrete Decoupling for Non-Density-Density Interactions in High-Pressure Hydride Superconductors
proposals.motivation: The recent surge in high-temperature superconductivity reports in hydrides under extreme pressure relies heavily on effective models with density-density interactions, decoupled via discrete Hubbard-Stratonovich transformations. However, realistic multi-orbital interactions, crucial for materials like LaH10, include spin-exchange and pair-hopping terms. The inability to efficiently incorporate these non-density-density terms limits the predictive power of computational studies and may contribute to the ongoing debate over the nature of superconductivity in these systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a generalized discrete decoupling scheme can be formulated for full Kanamori-type interactions, enabling large-scale quantum Monte Carlo simulations of realistic hydride models without a sign problem under specific conditions, thereby revealing the distinct superconducting signatures of spin-fluctuation-mediated pairing.
proposals.method: We will extend the discrete decoupling formalism, building on the work of Hirsch for density-density terms, to incorporate spin-flip and pair-hopping interactions. This involves identifying a new set of discrete auxiliary fields that couple to the relevant bilinears. The algorithm will be implemented within a determinant quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC) framework for a multi-orbital Hubbard model on a 3D lattice. We will benchmark the method against exact diagonalization for small clusters and apply it to a minimal two-orbital model parameterized for LaH10 under pressure, computing pairing susceptibilities and spin structure factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new computational tool to study realistic interaction Hamiltonians for pressurized hydrides, moving beyond the restrictive density-density approximation. It will directly assess the role of orbital physics and non-density interactions in stabilizing high-Tc superconductivity, offering a more rigorous theoretical basis to interpret and guide experimental claims in the field.
proposals.novelty: While discrete decoupling is well-established for density-density terms, its consistent extension to the full Kanamori interaction in a sign-problem-free manner for relevant geometries is an open challenge. Success here would unlock a new class of simulable models much closer to ab initio descriptions of candidate materials.

proposals.title: Monte Carlo Study of Interaction-Driven Superconductivity in Disordered, High-Pressure Hydride Analogs Using the Discrete Anderson-Hubbard Model
proposals.motivation: The recent surge in reports of high-temperature superconductivity in hydrides under extreme pressure has reignited debate over the role of disorder and electron-electron interactions.
