Pre-2026, the interplay of strong correlations and Anderson localization in these compressed, often disordered systems remains poorly understood, limiting theoretical predictions for material optimization. A controlled computational framework is needed to disentangle these effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the discrete transformation method, applied to an Anderson-Hubbard model, will reveal a non-monotonic relationship between disorder and the superconducting transition temperature (Tc), where moderate disorder can enhance pairing in regimes relevant to high-pressure hydrides.
proposals.method: We will implement large-scale determinant Quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC) simulations of a two-dimensional Anderson-Hubbard model with an attractive Hubbard interaction, representing the effective pairing interaction in hydrides. The discrete transformation for disorder will be employed to enable efficient sampling of large system sizes (e.g., 16x16 to 24x24 lattices) and a broad range of disorder strengths. Calculations will focus on measuring the superconducting pairing susceptibility and local density of states to map the phase diagram as a function of interaction strength, disorder, and chemical potential.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative phase diagram for superconductivity in the presence of correlated disorder and interactions, directly informing the interpretation of resistivity and magnetic screening data from high-pressure hydride experiments. It will establish whether disorder-induced localization can coexist with or even enhance superconductivity in parameter regimes suggested by first-principles calculations for these materials.
proposals.novelty: The novel synthesis applies Hirsch's discrete transformation—previously used for clean systems—specifically to the disordered, interacting problem, enabling large-scale simulations previously hindered by the sign problem in this regime. This directly connects abstract localization theory to the pressing, materials-specific questions in high-pressure superconductivity.

proposals.title: Inferring Microscopic Fermion Interactions from Effective Ising Models in High-Pressure Hydride Superconductors
proposals.motivation: The recent surge in reports of high-temperature superconductivity in hydrides under extreme pressure relies on interpreting experimental data (e.g., magnetic susceptibility drops) through effective Ising-type models for spin or orbital moments. However, the mapping from the underlying electronic Hamiltonian—with its complex fermion interactions, electron-phonon coupling, and Hund's rules—to these effective spin models is ambiguous. Resolving this inverse problem is crucial to distinguish between competing superconducting mechanisms (e.g., conventional electron-phonon vs. unconventional) in these materials.
proposals.hypothesis: The effective Ising coupling constants extracted from experimental magnetization or critical field data in hydride superconductors contain sufficient information to constrain the dominant microscopic fermion interaction parameters, allowing one to infer the relative strength of electron-phonon coupling versus electronic correlations.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational framework combining Density Functional Theory (DFT) for the electronic structure with a downfolding procedure to a low-energy multi-orbital Hubbard model. Using exact diagonalization and quantum Monte Carlo (pre-2026 methods like ALPS or TRIQS), we will compute the effective Ising couplings (J_ij) for various spin/orbital configurations as a function of the Hubbard U, Hund's J, and electron-phonon coupling strength λ. A machine learning regressor (e.g., a Random Forest or a shallow Neural Network) will then be trained on this synthetic dataset to perform the inverse mapping: from input Ising couplings (J_ij, local fields h_i) to output microscopic parameters (U, λ).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, data-driven tool to move beyond phenomenological Ising model fits in high-pressure superconductivity. It will offer a principled method to assess the dominant pairing glue from standard magnetization measurements, directly addressing controversies around confirmation bias in the field. The framework will be a generalizable contribution to the analysis of strongly correlated materials under pressure.
proposals.novelty: While the forward problem (deriving spin models from fermionic Hamiltonians) is standard, the systematic, quantitative inversion using pre-2026 computational many-body methods and machine learning to constrain microscopic parameters from effective model fits is novel. Applying this specifically to the contentious arena of hydride superconductors directly tackles a core interpretative challenge in the field.

proposals.title: Interchain Coupling and the Stability of the Peierls State in Quasi-One-Dimensional Systems: A Computational Study of the Peierls-Coulomb Competition
proposals.motivation: The competition between Peierls distortion (driving a charge density wave) and long-range Coulomb repulsion is a central paradigm for understanding electronic instabilities in quasi-one-dimensional materials. However, most theoretical treatments assume strict one-dimensionality, ignoring the weak but finite interchain coupling present in real materials. This coupling is crucial for understanding the observed temperature scales of transitions and the potential emergence of three-dimensional ordered states, such as superconductivity, in systems like organic charge-transfer salts and high-pressure hydrides where dimensional crossover is relevant.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that weak interchain electron hopping and Coulomb coupling will significantly modify the critical temperature and order parameter of the Peierls state, and may stabilize a superconducting ground state out of the competing Peierls-Coulomb landscape in a quasi-1D system.
proposals.method: We will extend the existing one-dimensional model for the Peierls-Coulomb competition (Hirsch, J.E., 2015, Phys. Rev. B 91, 184516) to two- and three-dimensional anisotropic lattices. The extended Hubbard model with electron-phonon coupling will be solved using determinant Quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC) algorithms to handle the fermionic sign problem at intermediate coupling. We will specifically vary the interchain hopping (t_perp) and the inter-site Coulomb interaction range to map the phase diagram as a function of temperature, pressure (modeled via bandwidth changes), and anisotropy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative phase diagram for competing orders in realistic quasi-1D materials, directly informing the interpretation of resistivity and magnetic susceptibility data in candidate systems. It will establish the role of dimensionality as a tuning parameter, analogous to pressure, in the Peierls-Coulomb framework, offering a new perspective on the search for high-temperature superconductivity in correlated low-dimensional materials.
proposals.novelty: While the 1D Peierls-Coulomb model is established, its systematic extension to study the explicit effects of two- and three-dimensional coupling on the competition's outcome is novel. This moves the theory from an idealized 1D limit to a regime directly applicable to real crystalline materials under high pressure.

proposals.title: Coulomb-Peierls Competition in Quarter-Filled and Commensurate Bands: Implications for High-Pressure Hydride Superconductors
proposals.motivation: The intense focus on high-pressure hydride superconductors has centered on half-filled band scenarios, where strong Coulomb repulsion and electron-phonon coupling compete. However, many candidate materials, including complex hydrides, may deviate from perfect half-filling due to chemical doping or structural complexity. The systematic analysis of this competition at other commensurate fillings, like quarter-filling, is lacking but crucial for a complete understanding of superconductivity and competing orders in these systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that at quarter-filling and other commensurate fillings, the phase diagram of the Hubbard-Holstein model will exhibit distinct regimes where superconductivity, charge density waves (CDW), and magnetism are stabilized, differing qualitatively from the well-studied half-filled case, with direct implications for the interpretation of high-pressure hydride data.
proposals.method: We will employ determinant Quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC) simulations for the two-dimensional Hubbard-Holstein model, extending our previous half-filling analyses. The method will specifically target quarter-filling (n=0.5) and other commensurate densities (e.g., n=0.25, 0.75). We will compute key correlation functions (pairing, charge, spin) and susceptibilities as functions of temperature, Coulomb interaction (U/t), and electron-phonon coupling (λ). The phase boundaries will be mapped by identifying divergences in these susceptibilities and analyzing finite-size scaling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive phase diagrams for the Coulomb-Peierls problem away from half-filling, directly applicable to the electronic structure of non-stoichiometric or doped hydrides. It will establish whether superconductivity is more or less robust at these fillings and clarify if reported resistance drops under pressure could be attributed to competing CDW phases rather than superconductivity, addressing the confirmation bias concern in the field.
proposals.novelty: While the half-filled Hubbard-Holstein model is a canonical problem, its systematic study at other commensurate fillings relevant to real materials is novel. This directly connects fundamental many-body theory to the ongoing debate over the nature of high-pressure hydride phenomena, moving beyond the default half-filled paradigm.

proposals.title: Mapping the Dimerization Phase Diagram in Correlated Materials: The Critical Role of Long-Range Coulomb Repulsion
proposals.motivation: The standard Hubbard model, with its on-site and nearest-neighbor interactions, is foundational for understanding correlated electron systems, including potential high-temperature superconductors. However, in materials like high-pressure hydrides where screening is debated, the assumption of fully screened long-range Coulomb forces may be invalid. A systematic study of the impact of second-neighbor and unscreened Coulomb repulsion on lattice instabilities is needed to clarify the electronic structure of these systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that including explicit second-neighbor and longer-range Coulomb repulsion will significantly alter the predicted dimerization and charge-ordering phase boundaries compared to the standard Hubbard model, revealing new insulating or preformed-pair phases relevant to the normal state of hydride superconductors.
proposals.method: We will employ exact diagonalization and density matrix renormalization group (DMRG) methods for one-dimensional chains and ladders, extending the standard Hubbard model with explicit Coulomb terms V1 (nearest-neighbor), V2 (second-neighbor), and a power-law decay V(r) ~ 1/r^α to model screening. The phase diagram will be mapped as a function of interaction strength (U, V1, V2, α), doping, and temperature by calculating charge structure factors, dimer correlations, and the charge gap. Benchmarking will be performed against known limits (e.g., extended Hubbard model at V2=0).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for assessing the stability of dimerized phases in materials with poor screening, directly informing the interpretation of resistivity and structural data in high-pressure hydrides. It will establish whether long-range Coulomb interactions can suppress or enhance tendencies toward charge ordering and pairing, thereby clarifying the electronic landscape from which superconductivity emerges.
proposals.novelty: While the extended Hubbard model (U, V1) is well-studied, a comprehensive phase diagram incorporating explicit second-neighbor and tunable long-range Coulomb repulsion, motivated by the specific screening conditions in hydride materials, is lacking and represents a significant advance beyond standard modeling approaches.

proposals.title: Thermal Melting of Competing Orders: A Finite-Temperature Phase Diagram for the 1D Peierls-Hubbard Model
proposals.motivation: The zero-temperature competition between Peierls dimerization and Coulomb-driven magnetism in one-dimensional systems is a cornerstone of correlated electron physics, relevant to organic conductors and novel quantum materials. However, the finite-temperature phase behavior, crucial for interpreting experimental resistivity and magnetic susceptibility data in real materials, remains poorly mapped. This gap limits our understanding of how thermal fluctuations mediate the transition between insulating states.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that thermal fluctuations will destabilize the long-range order of both the Peierls and Mott phases at distinct critical temperatures, creating an intermediate correlated metallic regime and significantly reshaping the quantum phase boundaries established at T=0.
proposals.method: We will employ determinant Quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC) simulations for the one-dimensional Peierls-Hubbard model, using the ALF (Algorithms for Lattice Fermions) implementation to avoid the sign problem at half-filling. Finite-temperature Lanczos methods will complement DQMC for smaller clusters to access spectral functions. The phase diagram will be constructed by calculating the temperature dependence of the charge-density-wave and spin-spin correlation functions, the Drude weight, and the compressibility across a range of electron-phonon coupling (λ) and Hubbard (U) strengths.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive finite-temperature (U, λ, T) phase diagram, quantifying the stability domains of insulating and metallic phases. It will provide concrete theoretical predictions for transport and magnetic measurements in candidate quasi-1D materials under ambient and high-pressure conditions, offering a new lens to analyze disputed superconducting transitions in correlated hydrides and organics.
proposals.novelty: While zero-temperature diagrams are common, a systematic finite-T study of the full Peierls-Hubbard model using large-scale, numerically exact quantum Monte Carlo is lacking. The explicit focus on thermal evolution directly addresses the interpretation of high-temperature experimental data in systems where electron-phonon and electron-electron interactions compete.

proposals.title: Interplay of Coulomb Repulsion and Lattice Frustration in the Spin-Peierls Transition of Quasi-One-Dimensional Quantum Magnets
proposals.motivation: The spin-Peierls transition, a cooperative spin-lattice instability, is a cornerstone of low-dimensional magnetism. While well-understood in simple antiferromagnetic chains, its fate in frustrated systems and under strong electronic correlations remains a key open question. This is crucial for interpreting the magnetic and structural properties of candidate quantum materials, including complex oxides and high-pressure hydrides where electron-phonon and electron-electron interactions compete.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that on-site (U) and nearest-neighbor (V) Coulomb repulsion will fundamentally alter the spin-Peierls phase boundary in frustrated spin chains, potentially stabilizing a dimerized state against frustration or leading to a novel competition with bond-order-wave or charge-density-wave states.
proposals.method: We will employ exact diagonalization and Density Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG) calculations on a frustrated, extended Hubbard model coupled to adiabatic phonons (a spin-Peierls model). The primary lattice will be a two-leg ladder or next-nearest-neighbor coupled chain to introduce frustration. We will compute spin and bond-order correlation functions, spin gaps, and lattice dimerization order parameters across the (U, V, frustration) parameter space, benchmarking against known limits.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a comprehensive phase diagram mapping the stability of the spin-Peierls state under strong correlations and frustration. It will deliver concrete theoretical predictions for experimental signatures, such as the evolution of the spin gap and lattice distortion with pressure—a tuning parameter for both electronic interactions and frustration. This directly informs the analysis of magnetic susceptibility and structural data in correlated low-dimensional materials.
proposals.novelty: Previous studies of the spin-Peierls transition largely treat electron-electron interactions within the pure Hubbard model (U only). The systematic inclusion of explicit nearest-neighbor repulsion (V) combined with deliberate geometrical frustration in the spin lattice is a novel synthesis, addressing a more realistic regime for many correlated materials.

proposals.title: Benchmarking the High-Tc Superconducting Phase in Compressed Hydrides with Auxiliary-Field Quantum Monte Carlo
proposals.motivation: The reported high-temperature superconductivity in hydrides like H3S and LaH10 under extreme pressure is a major pre-2026 breakthrough, but its theoretical description is challenging. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations for electron-phonon coupling are sensitive to approximations, and the role of dynamic Coulomb interactions beyond static screening remains debated. A method capable of treating electronic and phononic degrees of freedom with controlled accuracy is urgently needed to resolve these controversies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a fully quantum treatment of coupled electron-phonon-Coulomb interactions using Quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) will reveal that strong anharmonic phonon effects and dynamic Coulomb repulsion are both essential for accurately predicting the superconducting critical temperature (Tc) in high-pressure hydrides.
proposals.method: We will employ the Auxiliary-Field Quantum Monte Carlo (AFQMC) method, extended to include quantum phonons via the introduction of auxiliary fields for the phonon degrees of freedom. This will allow a numerically exact treatment of the coupled Hamiltonian. We will implement this for ab initio-derived effective low-energy models of H3S and LaH10, using constrained random phase approximation (cRPA) inputs for screened Coulomb parameters. The sign problem will be managed via the phaseless approximation, with benchmarks against diagrammatic Monte Carlo where feasible.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-accuracy, non-perturbative benchmarks for the superconducting gap and Tc in key hydride materials, directly testing the limits of DFT-based Migdal-Eliashberg theory. It will quantify the separate impacts of anharmonicity and dynamic Coulomb interactions, offering a definitive computational tool to assess future claims of high-Tc superconductivity in compressed materials.
proposals.novelty: While QMC is established for pure electronic systems, its direct application to fully coupled electron-phonon problems in realistic material models is a significant, non-trivial extension. This approach moves beyond the standard separation of electronic and lattice problems inherent in most pre-2026 methodologies.

proposals.title: Analytical Phase Diagram of Correlated Hydrides: A Bosonization and Renormalization Group Study of the Peierls-Mott Boundary
proposals.motivation: The recent surge in reports of high-temperature superconductivity in compressed hydrides has reignited debate over the fundamental electronic mechanisms at play. A key unresolved question is the competition between electron-phonon-driven Peierls/CDW states and electron-correlation-driven Mott insulating states, which delineates the optimal regime for superconductivity. An analytical, field-theoretic framework to map this phase boundary is critically needed to guide the search for new high-Tc materials beyond empirical pressure tuning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the one-dimensional Peierls-Mott transition in model systems for hydrogen chains or lattices can be mapped analytically using bosonization, and that the critical point and scaling exponents are accessible via a perturbative renormalization group (RG) flow analysis of the competing umklapp and electron-phonon coupling terms.
proposals.method: We will employ the standard g-ology framework for a 1D electron gas coupled to optical phonons. The fermionic Hamiltonian will be bosonized using the operator identity method. The RG flow equations for the coupling constants (backscattering g1, umklapp g3, and electron-phonon λ) will be derived at the one-loop level using the poor man's scaling approach. The phase boundary will be determined by numerically integrating these flow equations to identify the basins of attraction towards the Peierls (relevant λ) or Mott (relevant g3) fixed points, with the Luttinger parameter Kρ serving as the control variable.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a closed-form analytical expression for the Peierls-Mott phase boundary as a function of microscopic couplings (U, t, ωph, λ), offering a predictive tool for material design. It will establish a rigorous theoretical benchmark for interpreting resistivity and magnetic susceptibility data from high-pressure hydride experiments, helping to distinguish correlation effects from conventional phonon mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: While bosonization and RG are established for 1D Hubbard or Holstein models separately, their combined application to explicitly map the competitive phase boundary in a context directly relevant to high-pressure hydride superconductors is novel. This analytical approach contrasts with the prevailing reliance on numerical simulations (e.g., DQMC) for these systems.

proposals.title: Mapping the Interplay of Electron Correlation and Lattice Instability: A High-Pressure Phase Diagram of the Dual Mott-Peierls Transition
proposals.motivation: The competition between Mott (electron correlation-driven) and Peierls (electron-phonon-driven) insulating states is a fundamental, unresolved problem in correlated quantum materials. Understanding their phase boundary is crucial for disentangling mechanisms in high-pressure hydride superconductors, where both interactions are present but their relative dominance under pressure and temperature remains unclear. A systematic map of this phase space would provide a critical testbed for theories of unconventional superconductivity emerging near such quantum phase transitions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a well-defined, continuous phase boundary exists between the Mott and Peierls insulating states in a tunable model system, and that this boundary can be precisely mapped by controlling pressure and temperature, revealing a region of enhanced quantum fluctuations potentially conducive to superconductivity.
proposals.method: We will employ high-pressure diamond anvil cell techniques combined with in-situ electrical resistance and Raman spectroscopy measurements, as used in recent hydride superconductor studies. The model system will be a quasi-one-dimensional correlated material (e.g., an organic charge-transfer salt or a halogen-bridged transition metal chain) where both transitions are accessible. Pressure will tune electron correlation (U/W) and temperature will probe the phase stability. Data analysis will involve fitting transport data to activation laws and identifying spectroscopic signatures of dimerization to classify the insulating state.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive experimental phase diagram delineating the Mott and Peierls regimes in a single material. It will directly inform the interpretation of high-pressure hydride data by providing a benchmark for separating correlation and phonon signatures. The results will constrain theoretical models of competing orders and guide the search for superconductivity at their intersection.
proposals.novelty: While individual Mott or Peierls transitions are well-studied, systematically mapping their direct competition in pressure-temperature space in a single experiment is novel. This approach moves beyond simply identifying a superconducting state to fundamentally characterizing the landscape of competing insulating orders from which it may emerge.

proposals.title: Controlled Dimensional Crossover in Quasi-1D Quantum Materials via Tunable Inter-Chain Network Coupling
proposals.motivation: Understanding the dimensional crossover from 1D to higher-dimensional collective behavior is a fundamental problem in condensed matter physics, critical for interpreting phenomena in quasi-1D superconductors, spin chains, and Luttinger liquids. However, most theoretical treatments assume uniform inter-chain coupling, which is an oversimplification for real materials where disorder and complex connectivity exist. This gap limits our ability to predict and engineer the emergence of bulk properties from coupled 1D units.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dimensional crossover in quasi-1D systems is governed not just by the average inter-chain coupling strength, but critically by the spatial topology and heterogeneity of the coupling network, analogous to how functional integration in neural systems depends on specific long-range connectivity patterns.
proposals.method: We will perform large-scale quantum Monte Carlo and Density Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG) simulations on arrays of 1D spin-1/2 Heisenberg chains or Luttinger liquid wires. The inter-chain couplings will be modeled as a tunable network with parameters for strength, range (nearest-neighbor vs. long-range), and spatial disorder, inspired by connectivity matrices in network neuroscience. We will systematically vary these parameters to compute phase diagrams, measuring the evolution of spin correlation lengths, superconducting pairing correlations (for fermionic systems), and entanglement entropy scaling as functions of the coupling control parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, parameter-rich framework for understanding dimensional crossover, moving beyond the simple uniform coupling paradigm. It will deliver specific predictions for how coupling heterogeneity influences the threshold for 2D/3D order, offering new interpretative tools for experimental data on quasi-1D materials under pressure or in engineered heterostructures.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly importing the conceptual and modeling framework of neural network connectivity—where weak, specific long-range links drive functional integration—to the physics of coupled quantum chains, treating the coupling topology as a primary experimental variable rather than a fixed, uniform background.

proposals.title: From Spinless to Spinful Fermions: A Systematic Framework for Emergent Magnetism in Correlated Electron Models
proposals.motivation: Canonical spinless fermion models (e.g., t-V, spinless Hubbard) are widely used to study charge ordering and superconductivity in correlated materials, including high-pressure hydrides. However, they fundamentally ignore the spin-1/2 reality of electrons, precluding the description of magnetic phenomena crucial for a complete physical picture. This gap mirrors the historical evolution in molecular photophysics, where moving from spinless chromophore models to explicit singlet/triplet states was essential for predicting spin-dependent dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematically introducing spin degrees of freedom and relevant spin interactions (exchange, spin-orbit coupling) into established spinless fermion models will reveal new phases and critical points, fundamentally altering their phase diagrams and providing a more realistic pathway to model materials where magnetism and superconductivity compete.
proposals.method: We will start with the 2D spinless t-V and Hubbard models as baselines. The spin-1/2 degree of freedom will be formally introduced, expanding the orbital basis. We will then add perturbations: a Heisenberg exchange term (J) for nearest neighbors and a Rashba-type spin-orbit coupling term. The resulting unified Hamiltonian will be studied using exact diagonalization for small clusters and determinant Quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC) for larger systems, focusing on calculating spin-spin correlation functions, magnetic structure factors, and the modified charge-order/superconducting susceptibilities across temperature and interaction strength (U, V).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, adaptable framework for upgrading spinless models to physically complete spinful descriptions, directly applicable to modeling correlated hydrides and other quantum materials. It will map how magnetic order and spin textures emerge from purely charge-based models, offering new insights into the interplay between charge, spin, and orbital degrees of freedom in high-temperature superconductivity.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic, Hamiltonian-based adaptation of a conceptual advance from photophysics to condensed matter theory, explicitly constructing the pathway from spinless to spinful fermion models to study emergent magnetism, a step not typically formalized in existing literature.

proposals.title: Spinful Fermion Models for High-Pressure Hydride Superconductors: Magnetic Screening and Pairing Symmetry
proposals.motivation: The recent surge in reports of high-temperature superconductivity in hydrides under extreme pressure relies heavily on spinless fermion models (e.g., single-band Hubbard) to interpret magnetic field expulsion and electrical resistance drops. This simplification ignores the intrinsic spin degree of freedom, which is critical for understanding spin-dependent electron-phonon coupling, magnetic fluctuations, and the potential for unconventional pairing mechanisms beyond standard s-wave. A more realistic spinful description is urgently needed to distinguish genuine superconductivity from other correlated phenomena in these compressed materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating fermion spin into models of high-pressure hydrides will reveal significant spin-dependent renormalization of electron-phonon coupling and the emergence of competing magnetic orders, which can either enhance or suppress superconducting Tc and critically influence the Meissner screening profile.
proposals.method: We will extend existing spinless tight-binding and dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT) frameworks for hydride lattices (e.g., H3S, LaH10) to include explicit spin indices and on-site Hubbard U repulsion. The method will involve solving the spinful Hubbard-Holstein model using determinant Quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC) for finite-temperature properties and implementing spin-polarized DFT+DMFT calculations (using TRIQS/DFTTools) to compute spin-resolved spectral functions and magnetic susceptibilities under simulated pressure. Key observables will be the spin structure factor and the magnetic contribution to the pairing vertex.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic analysis of spin fluctuations and magnetic correlations in the high-pressure hydride superconductors, offering a theoretical basis for interpreting ambiguous magnetic screening data. It will establish whether spin physics supports or challenges the prevailing phonon-mediated pairing narrative, potentially identifying signatures of spin-triplet pairing or itinerant magnetism.
proposals.novelty: While spinless models dominate the high-pressure hydride literature, this study deliberately introduces spin to probe the interplay of magnetism and superconductivity—a factor largely neglected but central to Hirsch's critical analysis of these materials. The application of spin-resolved DMFT and DQMC to these specific hydride structures is novel.

proposals.title: Many-Body Localization in the Quasiperiodic Fermi-Hubbard Model: A Controlled Path from Thermalization to Localization
proposals.motivation: The existence of a true many-body localization (MBL) phase in the thermodynamic limit remains a central, unresolved question in quantum statistical mechanics. While experiments with ultracold atoms in bichromatic lattices have observed localization-like phenomena, theoretical understanding is muddled by the use of fully random disorder, which obscures the fundamental interplay between quasiperiodicity, interactions, and thermalization. A clean, deterministic model is needed to isolate the core physics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a one-dimensional Fermi-Hubbard model with a controlled, incommensurate onsite potential will exhibit a sharp, interaction-tuned transition from an ergodic to a many-body localized phase, characterized by a crossover in spectral statistics from Wigner-Dyson to Poisson and a halt in entanglement growth following a quantum quench.
proposals.method: We will perform large-scale exact diagonalization (ED) and time-evolving block decimation (TEBD) simulations on the quasiperiodic Fermi-Hubbard chain, H = -t∑⟨i,j⟩,σ (c†iσ cjσ + h.c.) + U∑i ni↑ni↓ + Δ∑i cos(2πβi + φ) ni, where β is the irrational golden ratio. We will compute the mean level spacing ratio ⟨r⟩, entanglement entropy dynamics, and imbalance decay to map the phase diagram in (Δ/U, U/t) space. The results will be directly formulated as predictions for ongoing experiments with ultracold ⁶Li or ⁴⁰K in optical superlattices.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, disorder-free theoretical benchmark for MBL in fermionic systems, clarifying the role of interactions in stabilizing localization against quasiperiodic potentials. It will yield specific, quantitative predictions for spectral and dynamical observables that can validate or challenge the MBL paradigm in current quantum simulation platforms.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a controlled, quasiperiodic potential—free from rare region effects of random disorder—to the interacting fermion problem most relevant to quantum gas experiments, thereby bridging a critical gap between abstract MBL theory and concrete, disorder-interaction-tuned quantum simulator studies.

proposals.title: Localization of Societal Impact in Quantum Materials Research: A Disorder-Weighted Framework
proposals.motivation: The societal translation of quantum materials research, such as high-pressure hydride superconductivity, is highly sensitive to volatile real-world factors like funding cycles, policy shifts, and public sentiment. Current impact metrics (e.g., citations, h-index) fail to account for this volatility, potentially misdirecting long-term research strategy. A framework that quantifies the resilience of research pathways to such 'disorder' is urgently needed to guide stable, high-benefit investment.
proposals.hypothesis: The propagation of societal impact in a research ecosystem can be modeled as a transport problem on a network, where the introduction of synthetic disorder (e.g., in resource access) leads to impact localization, analogous to Anderson localization in condensed matter. Research avenues with a high disorder-weighted impact index represent the most robust pathways for sustained societal benefit.
proposals.method: We will construct a network where nodes are specific research outcomes (e.g., a reported superconducting hydride) and edges represent pathways for societal translation (patents, policy documents, industry partnerships). Each node is assigned an 'impact potential' from altmetrics data (Dimensions.ai, Altmetric.com). Disorder is introduced by applying random, time-varying potentials to nodes, simulating funding or regulatory volatility. We will adapt the Anderson tight-binding model Hamiltonian, H = Σ_i ε_i |i⟩⟨i| + Σ_⟨ij⟩ t_ij |i⟩⟨j|, where ε_i represents the disordered impact potential and t_ij the translation coupling. The localization length of the system's eigenstates will be computed to derive the Disorder-Weighted Societal Impact Index (DWSII).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative model linking concepts of electronic localization to science policy, offering a new metric (DWSII) to evaluate research resilience. It will deliver a computational tool to identify which quantum materials sub-fields (e.g., hydride superconductors vs. topological insulators) are most likely to sustain societal benefit amidst volatility, informing funding agencies and institutional strategy.
proposals.novelty: While altmetrics and network science are established, applying the formal physics of disorder and localization to model the *robustness* of societal impact is a novel synthesis. This moves beyond descriptive impact tracking to a predictive framework grounded in the condensed matter physics expertise of the researcher.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Neural-Network and Density-of-States Quantum Monte Carlo Method for Fermion Thermodynamics in High-Pressure Hydrides
proposals.motivation: The theoretical understanding of high-temperature superconductivity in hydrides under pressure is severely limited by the fermion sign problem in quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) simulations. This prevents reliable computation of key thermodynamic signatures, such as specific heat and magnetic susceptibility, at relevant temperatures and system sizes, leaving critical questions about the pairing mechanism and magnetic response unresolved.
proposals.hypothesis: By decoupling the sampling of entropy via a global density-of-states method from the sampling of signed weights via a sign-constrained auxiliary-field solver, and by using a neural network state trained on finite-temperature ensembles to guide the latter, the sign problem can be systematically controlled to enable accurate fermion thermodynamics.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid algorithm combining the Wang-Landau density-of-states method to compute entropy with a sign-projection-stabilized auxiliary-field QMC (AFQMC) solver. A convolutional neural network (CNN) will be trained on finite-temperature AFQMC configurations to learn a guiding wavefunction that biases the auxiliary-field sampling towards sign-stable sectors. This CNN will be integrated as an importance-sampling function within the constrained-path or phaseless AFQMC framework, adaptively focusing computational effort.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first numerically stable framework for computing thermodynamic properties of realistic, multi-band fermion models for hydride superconductors at experimentally relevant temperatures. It will directly address controversies in the field by enabling unbiased calculations of magnetic susceptibility and specific heat to compare with high-pressure experiments.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the synergistic combination of a global entropy estimator with a locally sign-stabilized solver guided by a machine-learned finite-temperature state, a strategy not explored in pre-2026 QMC literature. This moves beyond static constraints or fixed-guiding-wavefunction approaches.

proposals.title: Mapping the Finite-N Phase Structure of the Gross-Neveu Model: Lattice Simulations and Resummed Expansions
proposals.motivation: The Gross-Neveu (GN) model is a foundational quantum field theory for studying dynamical symmetry breaking and dimensional transmutation, with its large-N limit providing exact analytical results. However, real-world systems like certain low-dimensional materials and cold atom setups are described by theories with a small, finite number of fermion flavors, where the applicability of the large-N approximation is uncertain. A systematic study of finite-N corrections is crucial to bridge this gap between idealized theory and experimental reality, potentially revealing new physics absent in the infinite-N limit.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that at moderate flavor numbers (N ~ 4-12), the GN model exhibits a qualitatively different phase structure compared to the large-N limit, featuring shifted critical couplings, intermediate phases, and non-monotonic evolution of critical exponents. The large-N limit's quantitative reliability is only achieved at unexpectedly large N.
proposals.method: We will employ high-precision lattice field theory simulations using staggered fermions in 2+1 dimensions, optimized for the GN model's four-fermion interaction. Simulations will be performed at specific, moderate N values (e.g., 4, 8, 12) to scan the coupling-temperature phase diagram. These numerical results will be combined with a Borel-Padé resummation of the known 1/N expansion to analytically control subleading effects and systematically compare with the lattice data, isolating genuine finite-N phenomena from artifacts.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-precision map of the GN model's phase trajectory from small to large N, definitively identifying the onset of large-N behavior. It will catalog any emergent finite-N phases and provide benchmark results for theorists and experimentalists working with low-dimensional correlated systems where N is inherently small.
proposals.novelty: While the large-N limit is textbook knowledge, a systematic, combined numerical-analytical investigation targeting the *transition region* between small-N and large-N physics is lacking. The search for intermediate phases and non-monotonic critical exponent flow at accessible N represents a novel, non-perturbative line of inquiry.

proposals.title: A Contribution-Weighted Benchmarking Protocol for Validating Quantum Monte Carlo Methods in High-Pressure Superconductivity
proposals.motivation: The computational study of high-pressure hydride superconductors relies heavily on Quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) methods, which require validation against exact or high-accuracy results. Currently, such benchmarking is often an unreported, private step, leading to wasted effort and a lack of standardized, trusted reference data. This creates reproducibility challenges and undervalues the essential work of method validation.
proposals.hypothesis: Formally attributing and quantifying the contribution of high-accuracy Density Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG) benchmark data to subsequent QMC studies will incentivize the creation of a public, citable benchmark repository, improve validation rigor, and provide a nuanced impact metric for methodological work.
proposals.method: We will develop a protocol where DMRG calculations on key, finite 2D Hubbard and t-J model clusters provide high-fidelity ground-state energies and correlation functions. These results will be published as a citable dataset with persistent identifiers. We will then track and formally quantify their use in validating new QMC algorithms (e.g., constrained-path AFQMC) applied to these models, assigning a 'contribution weight' based on the benchmark's role in confirming accuracy or diagnosing systematic error.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a framework to transform benchmark data into a credited scholarly product, encouraging its maintenance and reuse. It will provide a concrete measure of impact for computational methods beyond citation counts and enhance the reliability of computational studies on correlated electron systems relevant to superconductivity.
proposals.novelty: It moves beyond using benchmarks as a private tool to treating them as a public, incentivized resource. The formal quantification of a benchmark's 'contribution' to subsequent studies is a novel application of credit-sharing principles from authorship to methodological validation.

ideas.title: Finite-N Corrections and Emergent Phases in the Gross-Neveu Model: Beyond the Large-Flavor Limit
ideas.description: This research systematically investigates how finite fermion flavor number N qualitatively alters the phase structure of the Gross-Neveu model compared to its well-known large-N limit. Using high-precision lattice field theory simulations at strategically chosen moderate N values (e.g., N=4, 8, 12), it directly probes the phase diagram for evidence of intermediate phases or shifted critical couplings. These numerical results are combined with a resummed 1/N expansion to analytically control subleading effects, enabling the identification of any non-monotonic behavior or crossover phenomena in critical exponents as N increases. The goal is to map the precise trajectory of the theory from small-N to large-N, determining where the large-N limit becomes quantitatively and qualitatively reliable and cataloging any emergent physics that exists only at finite N.

ideas.title: Sign-Stabilized, Density-of-States Quantum Monte Carlo for Fermion Thermodynamics
ideas.description: This approach directly attacks the fermion sign problem at finite temperature by decoupling the sampling of entropy from the sampling of signed weights. It uses a global density-of-states method to compute the entropy, while a sign-constrained auxiliary-field solver, guided by a neural network state trained on finite-temperature ensembles, provides local estimates of the energy and other observables. This hybrid method systematically controls the sign problem by adaptively focusing computational resources on the most statistically relevant and sign-stable sectors of the Hilbert space. The result is a robust framework for computing thermodynamic properties like specific heat and magnetic susceptibility in interacting fermion models at previously inaccessible temperatures and system sizes.

ideas.title: Adapting Chromophore Engineering to Include Fermion Spin
ideas.description: This idea challenges the spinless fermion assumption by using an analogy from molecular photophysics. In that field, moving from simple chromophore models to spin-explicit configurations (singlets/triplets) was crucial for predicting spin-dependent processes like triplet energy transfer. The analogous solution involved expanding the orbital basis to include spin states and adding spin-dependent couplings to the Hamiltonian. Adapted here, the research would take a canonical spinless fermion model (e.g., the t-V or Hubbard model without spin) and formally introduce the spin-1/2 degree of freedom. By adding relevant spin interactions (Heisenberg exchange, spin-orbit coupling) as perturbations or through a unified Hamiltonian, one can study how magnetic ordering, spin textures, and spin-dependent correlation functions emerge and modify the original model's phase diagram, providing a more realistic pathway from simplified to physically complete models of interacting fermions.

ideas.title: Probe Many-Body Localization via Controlled Quasiperiodic Potentials in Interacting Fermion Models
ideas.description: This research program introduces deterministic, quasiperiodic potentials into interacting fermion models to study the precise mechanisms of many-body localization (MBL). By using a tunable, incommensurate lattice potential, it creates a clean platform where the strength of the localization-inducing field is precisely controlled, separating its effects from the complexities of fully random disorder. This allows for a systematic exploration of the phase boundary between thermal and MBL phases, the nature of the transition, and the stability of localization in the presence of interactions. The approach yields specific, testable predictions for spectral statistics, entanglement growth, and transport that can be directly compared with state-of-the-art quantum simulator experiments using ultracold atoms in bichromatic optical lattices.

ideas.title: Contribution-Weighted Benchmarking for Quantum Many-Body Methods
ideas.description: This research direction applies the principle of fractionalizing credit from authorship metrics to the validation of computational physics methods. Specifically, it develops a protocol where high-accuracy DMRG calculations are used not just as a passive benchmark for Monte Carlo methods, but as a citable reference resource. The 'contribution' of the benchmark result to subsequent Monte Carlo studies—such as validating a new algorithm or identifying a systematic error—is formally quantified and attributed. This creates an incentive structure for producing and maintaining high-quality benchmark data, transforms validation from a private step into a public, credited scholarly contribution, and provides a more nuanced measure of impact for methodological work in computational physics.

ideas.title: Probing Dimensional Crossover via Controlled Inter-Chain Coupling in Quasi-1D Systems
ideas.description: This idea challenges the strict 1D assumption by probing dimensional crossover in quasi-1D systems through the lens of an analogy from neuroscience/network science. In that field, the integration of functionally segregated neural regions via weak long-range connections is a core problem, solved by modeling the regions as coupled dynamical systems and varying inter-regional coupling to study synchronization and functional emergence. Adapted to condensed matter physics, the research involves simulating an array of weakly coupled 1D chains (e.g., spin chains or Luttinger liquids) not as a uniform quasi-1D lattice, but as a network of distinct 'chains' connected with a tunable and potentially non-uniform coupling scheme. The experimental protocol is to systematically vary the strength and spatial pattern (topology) of inter-chain couplings—minicking the study of long-range connectivity in neural systems—to precisely map how and at what threshold different physical properties (like spin correlation length, conductivity, or entanglement entropy scaling) cross over from strictly 1D behavior to 2D or 3D collective behavior. This approach focuses on the *control parameters* of the coupling itself as the primary lever to understand the transition, rather than just the end states.

ideas.title: Disorder-Weighted Societal Impact Index for Quantum Materials Research
ideas.description: This research direction applies the conceptual framework of disorder and localization from condensed matter physics to the science of science. It models a research ecosystem (e.g., quantum materials) as a system where individual projects or findings have an 'impact potential' measured by altmetrics (patents, policy mentions, industry collaborations). By introducing synthetic disorder—randomly varying parameters like funding accessibility, regulatory hurdles, or public attention—the framework studies how the overall societal impact signal localizes or propagates. This allows for the calculation of a disorder-weighted impact index, identifying which research avenues are most resilient to real-world volatility and thus offer the most stable path for long-term societal benefit.

ideas.title: Monte Carlo simulation of scientific career trajectories as interacting fermion systems at finite temperature
ideas.description: This work maps the problem of predicting future h-index onto a many-body fermion system, where each researcher occupies states in a space defined by topic, productivity, and collaboration network, subject to exclusion principles that prevent identical research niches from being simultaneously occupied. By extending imaginary-time fermion Monte Carlo methods to finite temperature, we sample thermodynamic ensembles of career trajectories where temperature encodes the level of stochastic uncertainty in a researcher's early career. The partition function and derived thermodynamic quantities (free energy landscape, susceptibility to perturbations like funding changes) yield probabilistic forecasts of future h-index that naturally incorporate competitive interactions among researchers and field-level phase transitions such as the emergence of hot topics. This physics-inspired framework goes beyond conventional regression or machine learning predictors by providing uncertainty-quantified ensemble forecasts grounded in a principled statistical mechanics formalism.

Based on these predictions, a targeted materials synthesis effort produces high-quality single crystals or thin films. These are then fabricated into devices incorporating strain gates, electrostatic dopants, or other tuning knobs that can precisely and reversibly modulate the effective inter-chain coupling strength. The system directly tests theoretical crossover predictions by correlating in-situ tuning with high-resolution transport, optical, or spectroscopic measurements, moving beyond generic simulation to a closed-loop, material-specific investigation of dimensional crossover.

ideas.title: Spin-Dependent Career-Length Bias in Scientific Impact Metrics for Condensed Matter Theory
ideas.description: This research direction investigates a systematic bias in academic impact metrics, like the h-index, arising from the methodological choice between computationally efficient spinless models and more realistic but complex spin-dependent models in condensed matter physics. It posits that the additional complexity of including spin degrees of freedom leads to longer project cycles, fewer publications per unit time, and delayed citation accumulation, mechanically disadvantaging early-career theorists in this subfield. The study would analyze publication and citation timelines to empirically test whether researchers focusing on spinless models accumulate metrics faster, creating an unfair comparative advantage independent of scientific merit. This reveals a hidden 'methodological career-length bias' where the pursuit of physical realism interacts perversely with incentive structures, potentially steering research away from important but computationally intensive questions.

ideas.title: Renormalization-Group-Guided Citation Dynamics for Accelerating Scientific Discovery
ideas.description: This idea applies the conceptual framework of the renormalization group (RG) to the meta-scientific process of methodological citation and development. The paper-level h-index, based on citing authors' frequency of use, serves as a community-wide measure of an algorithm's 'relevance' at different scales of simulation. By analyzing the citation network and h-index dynamics of papers on RG-improved Monte Carlo methods, one can construct a flow in 'method-space' that identifies which algorithmic refinements are being widely adopted and are thus most impactful for tackling specific critical phenomena. This creates a feedback loop where the scientific community's collective usage data guides the strategic allocation of future research effort towards the most promising RG transformations, systematically accelerating convergence in challenging computational physics problems.

ideas.title: Gauge-invariant meron-cluster algorithm for lattice gauge theories with dynamical fermions
ideas.description: This approach reformulates the lattice gauge theory with dynamical fermions in a direct-space, imaginary-time path integral representation where fermions are represented by worldlines and gauge fields by discrete plaquette occupation numbers. The core innovation is a cluster algorithm that groups fermionic worldlines into gauge-invariant, signed clusters (merons) whose contributions can be evaluated analytically. This isolates and eliminates the primary source of the sign problem arising from fermion-gauge coupling. The remaining configuration space, consisting of bosonic gauge field fluctuations and worldline shapes for neutral clusters, has a positive-definite weight, enabling efficient simulation with controlled systematic errors.

ideas.title: Quantum-Validated Real-Time Dynamics via Imaginary-Time Constraints
ideas.description: This approach bypasses the ill-posed analytic continuation step by using a near-term quantum device to compute real-time correlation functions directly via variational quantum simulation. The quantum circuit's ansatz is trained classically to reproduce accurate imaginary-time correlation data obtained from high-precision Monte Carlo, imposing a stringent physical constraint. This hybrid validation ensures the real-time output is both consistent with exact imaginary-time benchmarks and directly computed from a unitary evolution, eliminating the instability of inversion. The result is a robust, first-principles framework for dynamical properties that leverages quantum computation where it is naturally suited while grounding it in classically verifiable data.

ideas.title: Path-integral molecular dynamics inspired finite-temperature fermion Monte Carlo
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy between the challenge of extending ground-state imaginary-time fermion Monte Carlo to finite temperature and the analogous problem solved in computational chemistry when path-integral molecular dynamics (PIMD) extended zero-temperature electronic structure methods to capture finite-temperature nuclear quantum effects. In PIMD, each quantum particle is mapped onto a ring polymer of P classical beads in the imaginary-time direction, with P inversely proportional to temperature, and efficient sampling is achieved through staging transformations, colored-noise thermostats (PIGLET), and ring-polymer contraction schemes. The adapted approach applies this ring-polymer discretization to fermionic worldlines in lattice or continuum models: each fermion trajectory in imaginary time [0, β] is represented as a closed ring polymer, and Monte Carlo moves update bead positions using staging or Fourier-mode sampling borrowed from PIMD. The fermion sign problem—absent in the bosonic nuclear case—is handled by imposing restricted-path constraints at each imaginary-time slice, extending the fixed-node approximation from ground-state diffusion Monte Carlo to the thermal density matrix. Variance reduction techniques from PIMD, such as virial estimators for energy and pressure, are adapted to compute thermodynamic observables (internal energy, entropy, specific heat) of interacting fermion systems. This cross-disciplinary transfer offers a systematic framework for finite-temperature fermion simulations that leverages decades of algorithmic refinement in the molecular dynamics community.

ideas.title: Entropy of citation flows across coupled disciplinary chains
ideas.description: This fused approach models the academic citation network as a system of quasi-one-dimensional chains, where each chain represents a discipline or subfield and weak inter-chain couplings represent cross-disciplinary citations. Shannon entropy is computed on citation distributions both within and across these coupled chains, enabling detection of dimensional crossover effects—points at which a researcher's or field's citation pattern transitions from narrowly confined (strictly 1D, single-discipline) to broadly dispersed (higher-dimensional, interdisciplinary). By borrowing the coupled-chain framework from condensed matter physics, the method provides a principled way to quantify when and how interdisciplinary knowledge transfer emerges, identify researchers at the crossover threshold, and distinguish genuine interdisciplinarity from superficial citation scattering. This yields a richer diagnostic than standard citation entropy by encoding the topological structure of disciplinary coupling into the entropy measure itself.

ideas.title: Monte Carlo sampling of citation networks to identify phase transitions in researcher impact
ideas.description: This work reframes citation networks as lattice systems where researchers occupy sites and citations act as couplings, then applies imaginary-time path integral Monte Carlo methods to sample the partition function of this academic impact landscape. By treating the inverse h-index (citations-per-paper for low-output researchers) as an order parameter analogous to a lattice field observable, the framework can identify critical thresholds and phase transitions separating qualitatively different impact regimes—such as the boundary between efficient high-impact contributors and prolific but diluted publishers. The Monte Carlo sampling naturally handles the complex correlations in citation data (co-authorship, field effects, temporal dynamics) that confound simple bibliometric indicators, while the lattice gauge theory machinery provides a principled way to enforce gauge symmetries corresponding to field-normalization and self-citation invariance. This fusion produces a rigorous statistical-mechanical toolkit for bibliometric analysis that goes beyond ranking researchers, instead revealing the emergent collective structure of scientific impact.

ideas.title: Temporal Trajectories of Scientific Impact in Large-N Theoretical Models
ideas.description: This research applies the concept of time-dependent h-index tracking to the evolution of theoretical physics models themselves, using the Gross-Neveu model as a case study. Instead of tracking a researcher's career, we track the model's 'impact trajectory' in the literature—measuring citation accumulation and derivative work—as the flavor number N is increased toward the large-N limit, a process analogous to the passage of theoretical 'time'. This reveals whether certain theoretical limits or phase transitions lead to a rapid, early peak in influence or a sustained, late-blooming growth in scientific utility. The synergy provides a quantitative, bibliometric lens on theoretical development, connecting formal mathematical limits to tangible patterns in the collective research enterprise.

ideas.title: Analytic Continuation of Scholarly Impact Trajectories: Predicting Long-Range H-Index Dynamics from Noisy Early-Career Data
ideas.description: This work reframes the prediction of a researcher's future h-index as an ill-posed inversion problem analogous to recovering real-frequency spectral functions from noisy imaginary-time quantum Monte Carlo data. A scholar's early publication record (citations over discrete time bins) is treated as a sampled correlator on a limited domain, and maximum entropy or stochastic analytic continuation methods are applied to extrapolate the full career impact trajectory, naturally regularizing against overfitting to noisy early signals. The approach introduces a principled Bayesian framework — already battle-tested in condensed matter physics for handling sparse, noisy data with broad spectral features — that yields not just point estimates of future h-index but full posterior distributions capturing prediction uncertainty. By bridging computational physics methodology with scientometrics, this fusion enables robust career impact forecasting that gracefully handles the data-poor regime of early-career researchers where conventional regression models are most fragile.

ideas.title: Citation Surplus Score: Identifying Underrecognized High-Impact Researchers via Field-Normalized Excess Citation Analysis
ideas.description: We propose the Citation Surplus Score (CSS), a bibliometric indicator that identifies researchers whose papers consistently attract citations far exceeding field- and career-stage-adjusted expectations, even when their total publication count is modest. Unlike the naive inverse h-index, CSS regresses out confounding factors including venue prestige, co-author prominence, paper type, and publication age, then measures the per-paper excess citation mass above the predicted baseline. A minimum publication threshold and exclusion of reviews and retracted work prevent gaming and statistical artifacts. We validate CSS longitudinally by testing whether high-CSS researchers subsequently produce impactful work, establishing it as a predictive talent-identification metric rather than a retrospective curiosity.

ideas.title: Lattice-based Monte Carlo simulation of citation accumulation dynamics for detecting career-length bias in h-index
ideas.description: This work adapts imaginary-time Monte Carlo methods on two-dimensional lattice structures to model the stochastic dynamics of citation accumulation across scholarly careers. Each lattice site represents a publication, with inter-site couplings encoding citation flow probabilities that evolve over simulated career time, analogous to fermion hopping on a 2D lattice. By running large-scale simulations with controlled career-length parameters while holding intrinsic research quality constant, the framework can isolate the mechanical amplification effect of longer careers on the h-index from genuine productivity differences. This lattice simulation approach provides a principled, physics-inspired null model for quantifying h-index career-length bias, offering the bibliometrics community a tool that goes beyond retrospective regression analysis to deliver controlled counterfactual estimates of fairness violations.

ideas.title: Dynamical Correlation Functions via Bayesian Seismic Inversion
ideas.description: This idea proposes to compute real-time dynamical response functions from imaginary-time Monte Carlo data by drawing an analogy to seismic tomography in geophysics. In seismic tomography, Bayesian inference is used to reconstruct the Earth's interior from incomplete, noisy surface wave data. Adapted to the quantum many-body problem, the imaginary-time Monte Carlo data is treated as the indirect, noisy measurement. The goal is to invert for the real-frequency spectral function, an ill-posed problem. The solution is to implement a Bayesian analytic continuation framework, where a likelihood function based on the Monte Carlo data is combined with a prior probability distribution encoding physical constraints (e.g., non-negativity, known moments). This yields the most probable real-time correlation function and a quantitative estimate of the uncertainty inherent in the continuation process, providing a more robust and interpretable result than direct inversion methods.

ideas.title: Counterfactual cohort-matched h-index fairness: isolating career-length bias from productivity confounds
ideas.description: This study develops a causal inference framework to disentangle career-length bias in the h-index from correlated confounds such as publication rate, field-specific citation dynamics, collaboration network growth, and temporal inflation in citation counts. By constructing matched cohorts of researchers with equivalent career lengths, fields, and productivity profiles, we estimate counterfactual h-index trajectories and compute residual fairness scores that reveal the pure mechanistic advantage conferred by longer careers. The framework employs propensity score matching and doubly robust estimation across a large-scale bibliometric dataset spanning multiple disciplines and career-start decades. This approach overcomes the fundamental limitation of naive time-normalization by quantifying exactly how much of the h-index gap between senior and junior researchers is attributable to time alone versus genuine differences in research impact.

ideas.title: Multi-resolution continuum framework with controlled regulator removal for correlation functions
ideas.description: This idea develops a computational continuum formulation designed to systematically eliminate regulator dependence, addressing the core flaw that any numerical method introduces a discretization. It uses adaptive multi-resolution bases (e.g., wavelets) to represent fields, explicitly treating the basis cutoff as a regulator akin to the lattice spacing. The framework includes a rigorous procedure for continuum extrapolation by analyzing the regulator-dependence of correlation functions. The method is first benchmarked against high-precision lattice results in lower-dimensional or solvable models to validate the extrapolation procedure, then applied to compute specific correlation functions in QCD-like theories where lattice artifacts are significant, aiming for a robust, regulator-independent result.

ideas.title: Disorder-induced localization transitions in citation networks as a model for intellectual isolation
ideas.description: This work maps citation networks onto tight-binding lattice models where papers are sites, citations are hopping terms, and network centrality eigenvalues parallel energy spectra. By introducing tunable disorder—modeled as random perturbations to citation weights representing institutional barriers, language gaps, or disciplinary silos—we study whether researcher impact undergoes Anderson localization transitions, becoming exponentially confined to small network neighborhoods rather than propagating globally. The approach yields a novel 'localization length' metric for scholarly impact that quantifies how far a researcher's influence actually penetrates versus how far standard PageRank suggests it should, revealing hidden fragility in seemingly high-impact profiles. This fusion provides both a new diagnostic tool for scientometrics and a fresh physical system for studying localization phenomena on complex real-world networks with non-trivial degree distributions.

ideas.title: Continuum Credit Attribution for Scientific Collaborations
ideas.description: This research direction applies the conceptual shift from discrete lattices to continuum limits—used in physics to eliminate discretization artifacts—to the problem of scientific credit assignment. It challenges the discrete, artifact-laden model where each coauthor in a list receives equal, binary credit for a paper's citations. Instead, it proposes formulating author contribution as a continuous field or distribution over the intellectual space of a research project. By developing mathematical frameworks to fractionalize citations based on this continuum of contribution, the fusion aims to produce correlation functions (networks and metrics of credit) free from the distortions introduced by the arbitrary 'lattice' of an author list, leading to a more fundamental and accurate theory of scientific recognition.

ideas.title: Topic-resolved citation normalization via hierarchical bibliometric embeddings
ideas.description: This system computes a normalized researcher impact index by estimating expected citation rates at the research-topic level rather than the coarse discipline level. Each paper is assigned to a fine-grained topic cluster using a combination of citation network community detection and semantic embeddings of abstracts, and its citation count is normalized against the median citation trajectory of papers in that same cluster and publication year. The per-paper normalized scores are then aggregated into a researcher-level index analogous to the h-index but robust to cross-subfield variation. This hierarchical approach eliminates the homogeneity assumption that undermines field-level normalization, enabling fair comparison not only across disciplines but also across subfields with dramatically different citation cultures.

ideas.title: Replace Lattice Discretization with a Continuum Spectral Method
ideas.description: This idea challenges the foundational assumption of lattice discretization in numerical field theory by drawing an analogy from Computational Fluid Dynamics. In CFD, moving from body-fitted structured grids to continuum spectral methods solved the problem of mesh-induced artifacts. Adapting this solution, we propose to replace the discrete spacetime lattice in field theory simulations with a continuum formulation using a spectral decomposition. Quantum fields would be expanded in a complete set of continuous, smooth basis functions defined over the entire spacetime manifold. This approach directly enforces the continuum limit, preserves continuous symmetries like rotation and translation by construction, and aims to computationally eliminate the lattice artifacts that currently plague the calculation of correlation functions.

ideas.title: Adapting Financial Wash-Trading Detection to Identify and Correct Self-Citation Inflation in the H-Index
ideas.description: Strategic self-citation to inflate the h-index mirrors the well-studied problem of wash trading in financial markets, where actors execute circular transactions to artificially boost volume and price metrics. Financial regulators solved this through network surveillance algorithms that detect self-dealing loops, statistical outlier detection for abnormal transaction ratios, and the computation of adjusted metrics that exclude or down-weight fraudulent activity. We adapt this framework to bibliometrics by treating the citation network as a financial transaction graph: each citation is a transaction, and self-citations are potential wash trades. The adapted approach involves three components: (1) a graph-based detection module that identifies self-citation clusters and circular citation rings (analogous to wash-trade surveillance), using temporal burst analysis to flag periods of abnormal self-citation activity; (2) a statistical benchmarking system that computes field-normalized self-citation ratios and flags authors whose self-citation patterns deviate significantly from disciplinary norms (analogous to broker-dealer compliance thresholds); and (3) a corrected metric, h-index_adj, that applies a discount function to self-citations based on their likelihood of being strategic rather than substantive, similar to how adjusted trading volume excludes wash trades. The discount function would weigh factors such as whether the self-citation introduces genuinely new content, the temporal proximity of self-citations to index thresholds, and the proportion of self-citations relative to field baselines. This approach preserves the legitimate role of self-citation in building on one's own work while providing a more robust measure of scholarly impact.

ideas.title: Analytic continuation of citation dynamics for field-invariant impact spectra
ideas.description: This approach treats a researcher's citation record as a noisy discrete signal analogous to imaginary-time correlation data in quantum Monte Carlo, where the observed citation counts at discrete time points are an incomplete projection of an underlying continuous impact response function. By applying maximum-entropy and Padé analytic continuation methods to invert citation time-series into continuous spectral functions of scholarly impact, we recover field-characteristic citation decay kernels—fast-decaying modes in rapidly publishing fields like biomedicine versus slow-decaying modes in mathematics. These reconstructed spectral densities provide a principled, physics-motivated normalization basis for the h-index: instead of dividing by crude field averages, we normalize each researcher's impact by the spectral weight distribution typical of their discipline, enabling cross-field comparisons that respect the intrinsic temporal dynamics of knowledge diffusion. The fusion yields a framework that neither static field-normalization nor analytic continuation alone can provide—a dynamical, noise-robust, and theoretically grounded metric for equitable scholarly evaluation across disciplines.

ideas.title: Tomographic decomposition strategy for 2D fermion Monte Carlo inspired by CT image reconstruction
ideas.description: Extending direct-space imaginary-time Monte Carlo from one-dimensional to two-dimensional fermion lattices faces the notorious fermion sign problem, which grows exponentially worse with system size in higher dimensions. An instructive analogy comes from medical computed tomography (CT), where the challenge of reconstructing a full 2D image from inherently limited measurement data was solved by the Radon-transform framework: the 2D object is decomposed into sets of 1D projections taken along different angles, each projection is processed independently using filtered back-projection or iterative algebraic reconstruction (ART/SART), and the 1D results are combined to recover the 2D structure with controlled error. Adapting this strategy, one can decompose a 2D fermion lattice into families of 1D chains oriented along the principal lattice directions (e.g., rows and columns of a square lattice). The well-established 1D imaginary-time Monte Carlo is applied along each chain, where sign-problem severity is manageable and the method produces reliable Green's functions and correlation data. The transverse (inter-chain) hopping and interaction terms are then incorporated iteratively: after each directional sweep of 1D Monte Carlo runs, the inter-chain contributions update effective fields or sampling weights for the next sweep, analogous to how CT reconstruction iteratively refines the 2D image from successive projection updates. Convergence criteria borrowed from iterative reconstruction—residual error norms and ordered-subset acceleration—provide a principled framework for deciding when the 2D solution is self-consistent. This tomographic decomposition approach offers a practical pathway to 2D fermion simulations by leveraging the computational tractability of 1D Monte Carlo while systematically building in two-dimensional correlations.

ideas.title: Size-Normalized Fractional H-Index for Fair Cross-Institutional Research Comparison
ideas.description: This work proposes a fractional, size-normalized extension of the h-index for research groups and institutions that overcomes the dominant size-bias flaw of naive group-level aggregation. Each publication's citation credit is distributed across affiliated institutions proportional to fractional author contributions, and citations are field-normalized using reference baselines to ensure cross-disciplinary fairness. The resulting metric is expressed per active-researcher FTE, enabling meaningful comparisons between a 50-person department and a 5,000-person university system. We validate the metric on multi-decade publication records from institutions spanning diverse sizes and fields, demonstrating that it successfully decorrelates measured impact from institutional headcount while preserving sensitivity to genuine research excellence.

ideas.title: Embedding-weighted topical h-index with soft relevance scoring
ideas.description: This metric computes a researcher's depth of expertise within subfields by replacing brittle hard topic assignments with continuous relevance scores derived from paper embeddings in a semantic space. Each paper's contribution to a topic-specific h-index is weighted by its cosine similarity to a topic centroid learned from a stable reference corpus, eliminating the binary classification errors that plague keyword- or clustering-based approaches. The soft-weighted h-index is defined as the largest value k such that k papers each have at least k citations and a topic relevance score above a calibrated threshold, providing a reproducible and manipulation-resistant measure. Temporal drift in topic boundaries is handled through incremental centroid updates anchored to landmark papers, ensuring cross-era comparability.
