ideas.title: Trace Element Enrichment Analysis Applied to Scholarly Impact: Identifying High-Concentration, Low-Volume Researchers
ideas.description: This idea adapts the enrichment factor concept from economic geology and geochemistry to bibliometrics. In geochemistry, the enrichment factor quantifies how concentrated a trace element is in a sample relative to its background crustal abundance, allowing geologists to identify small but extraordinarily valuable ore deposits that bulk-volume measures would overlook. Analogously, we propose a Citation Enrichment Factor (CEF) defined as (mean citations per paper for researcher R) divided by (median citations per paper in field F), further scaled by an inverse volume penalty such as log(N_field / N_researcher) where N represents publication count. Researchers with very few publications but citations-per-paper far exceeding field norms would exhibit high CEF values, much like a narrow quartz vein showing gold concentrations thousands of times above crustal average. The metric naturally handles field normalization (since it is a ratio to field median), penalizes inflated self-citation (through optional citation-quality filters analogous to assay purity controls), and provides an intuitive classification scheme: just as mining engineers use ore grade cutoffs to decide which deposits merit extraction, research evaluators could use CEF thresholds to identify overlooked high-impact researchers who deserve funding, mentorship, or collaboration despite slim publication records. This inverse h-index alternative directly addresses the bias in conventional metrics toward prolific publishers and offers a complementary lens for talent identification in science policy.

ideas.title: Topic-stratified citation survival analysis for measuring expertise durability
ideas.description: This approach applies survival analysis techniques to citation trajectories of papers grouped by research topic, then constructs a durability-weighted micro h-index that captures both depth of output and longevity of influence within each subfield. Standard micro h-index treats all cited papers equally regardless of whether their citations peaked and decayed quickly or sustained over decades; by modeling topic-specific citation survival curves, we can distinguish researchers whose subfield contributions have enduring impact from those with high but transient productivity. The method fits Cox proportional hazard or accelerated failure time models to within-topic citation streams, estimating a per-topic citation half-life for each researcher, which is then integrated with the micro h-index to produce a durability-adjusted expertise score. This fusion enables finer-grained faculty evaluation, grant review, and collaboration matching by identifying scholars who are not merely prolific in a niche but whose niche contributions demonstrably shape the field over time.

ideas.title: Field-normalized national research excellence and equity benchmarking system
ideas.description: This system benchmarks national research competitiveness by computing two complementary, size-independent indicators per country and scientific domain: the proportion of publications falling in the top decile of field-normalized citation distributions (excellence rate) and a Gini coefficient measuring how concentrated research output is across institutions within the country (equity index). By normalizing citations within fields before aggregation, the system eliminates the disciplinary bias that makes raw h-index comparisons meaningless across domains like mathematics versus biomedicine. The dual-indicator design enables science policymakers to distinguish genuinely excellent national research ecosystems from those that merely have large output volumes or rely on a single dominant institution, providing actionable intelligence for funding allocation and international collaboration strategy.

ideas.title: Ecosystem Biodiversity Indices as a Model for Collective Research Impact Metrics
ideas.description: The h-index was designed for individual researchers, and naively summing or averaging individual h-indices to characterize groups produces misleading results—large departments appear stronger simply due to size, and internal co-citations inflate counts. Ecology confronted an analogous problem: measuring biodiversity across nested spatial scales without conflating local richness with regional accumulation. Whittaker's alpha-beta-gamma diversity framework solved this by decomposing total diversity into within-site (alpha), between-site (beta), and regional (gamma) components. Adapting this approach, we propose a hierarchical h-index decomposition for research collectives. The alpha component is the standard individual h-index distribution within a group. The beta component quantifies collaborative interaction effects—measuring how much citation impact arises from cross-member collaboration versus independent work, analogous to species turnover between ecological sites. The gamma component is the group-level h-index computed on the union of all publications, adjusted by a correction factor derived from internal co-citation networks to prevent double-counting. This framework enables meaningful comparisons across institutions of varying sizes, reveals whether collective impact is driven by individual excellence or synergistic collaboration, and can be recursively applied from lab groups to departments to universities to national research systems, mirroring the spatial scaling properties of ecological diversity indices.

ideas.title: Dual-lens citation portfolio profiling via Gini-Entropy decomposition
ideas.description: This approach jointly applies the Gini coefficient and Shannon entropy to a researcher's citation distribution, mapping each scholar into a Gini-Entropy plane that captures both inequality and diversity simultaneously. While Gini quantifies how unevenly citations concentrate across papers, entropy measures the informational complexity of the distribution shape, and their combination reveals structural archetypes — such as 'one-hit wonders' versus 'broad contributors' versus 'niche specialists' — that neither metric can isolate independently. By analyzing the theoretical and empirical relationship between these two measures, including boundary cases and their joint trajectories over a career, the framework provides a richer diagnostic tool for evaluating portfolio balance and predicting future impact patterns. The dual-metric space also enables principled clustering of researchers and detection of anomalous citation profiles that may signal emerging influence or field-specific publication norms.

ideas.title: Applying progressive income tax bracket logic to citation-based scholarly impact metrics
ideas.description: The h-index treats all papers with at least h citations identically and completely ignores citation counts beyond h, creating an insensitivity to highly-cited breakthrough work. An analogous problem exists in public economics: a flat tax or a hard income cap ignores the differential capacity of high earners, producing inequitable or information-losing aggregate measures. The well-established solution is the progressive tax bracket system, where income is partitioned into ranges and each range is weighted at a different marginal rate, ensuring that extreme values are neither ignored nor allowed to dominate the total. Adapting this mechanism to bibliometrics, we propose a 'progressive citation index' that replaces the h-index's hard ceiling with a bracketed weighting scheme. For each paper, citations up to h count fully (weight 1.0), citations from h+1 to 2h count at a reduced weight (e.g., 0.5), and citations beyond 2h count at a further reduced weight (e.g., 0.25). The researcher's score becomes the sum of these weighted contributions across all papers meeting the h threshold. This preserves the h-index's robustness to inflation from a single outlier paper while introducing graded sensitivity to truly exceptional work—mirroring how progressive taxation balances equity and incentive. The bracket boundaries and weights can be calibrated empirically per discipline using citation distribution data, analogous to how tax brackets are set based on income distributions.

ideas.title: Provenance-Verified Societal Impact Index with Adversarial Filtering
ideas.description: This system computes a researcher's societal impact by tracking verified uptake of their work across policy documents, patents, clinical guidelines, educational materials, and curated media coverage, rather than relying on raw altmetric counts that are easily gamed. Each impact channel is weighted by epistemic distance from the original finding and normalized against field-specific baselines to ensure fair cross-disciplinary comparison. An adversarial filtering layer employs anomaly detection to identify and discount manipulation patterns such as coordinated social media campaigns or self-referential policy citations. The resulting index provides a robust, manipulation-resistant measure of how research actually changes practice, policy, and technology beyond academia.

ideas.title: Radioactive decay models for citation obsolescence dynamics
ideas.description: This research draws an analogy between radioactive decay in nuclear physics and citation obsolescence in bibliometrics. In nuclear physics, the decay of unstable isotopes is modeled using multi-component exponential decay models and Bateman equations that describe sequential decay chains where parent isotopes produce daughter products, each with characteristic half-lives. Competing decay channels allow physicists to determine which mechanism dominates an isotope's transformation. We adapt this framework to model citation dynamics by treating each paper's citation stream as a superposition of multiple decay processes: a fast-decaying component capturing ephemeral methodological or trend-driven citations, and a slow-decaying component representing lasting foundational impact. The Bateman equation analogy is extended to citation cascades, where a paper's citations propagate through secondary and tertiary citing works, each generation exhibiting its own decay constant. A competing risks survival model distinguishes whether citation decline results from genuine knowledge obsolescence, active supersession by improved methods or findings, or passive field-level contraction. This multi-channel decay approach yields richer half-life estimates than single-exponential models, enabling classification of papers into decay-type categories (flash-in-the-pan, sleeping beauty, steady-state classic) and providing actionable metrics for research evaluation that capture the qualitative character of a paper's long-term influence, not merely its duration.

ideas.title: Niche Breadth Indices Applied to Topic-Level Scholarly Productivity
ideas.description: This idea adapts ecological niche breadth analysis to bibliometrics. In community ecology, Levins' niche breadth index and related specialization measures decompose an organism's total abundance into contributions across discrete resource dimensions, distinguishing habitat generalists from specialists. Analogously, we define a micro h-index by restricting the standard h-index calculation to publications within a single research topic (identified via keyword clustering, journal classification, or topic modeling). For each scholar, we compute a vector of micro h-indices across all their active topics. Comparing these niche-level scores to the aggregate h-index produces a specialization profile akin to an ecological niche breadth diagram. A scholar with one dominant micro h-index is a topical specialist; one with several comparable micro h-indices is a generalist. This dual-scaling framework preserves the interpretability of the h-index while adding granular, topic-resolved information about where a researcher's depth truly lies—enabling fairer cross-field comparisons, more targeted reviewer matching, and richer models of scholarly career trajectories.

ideas.title: Context-Aware Citation Decomposition with Anti-Prestige-Bias Calibration
ideas.description: This system moves beyond simple impact-weighted citation counts by decomposing each citation event into its rhetorical function—foundational, methodological, contrastive, or ceremonial—using NLP-based classification of citation contexts. Each function receives a distinct weight reflecting its epistemic significance, so a paper that is critically engaged with contributes more signal than one cited as a perfunctory nod to the field. To counteract the rich-get-richer bias inherent in impact-based weighting, citing-paper influence is normalized against field-specific and age-cohort baselines, ensuring that a meaningful citation from an early-career researcher in a small subfield is not systematically discounted. The system is validated through adversarial benchmarks that test whether it correctly identifies historically recognized cases of delayed recognition, sleeping beauties, and paradigm shifts that conventional weighted-citation metrics would miss.

ideas.title: Citation-context-aware heterogeneous network centrality for disentangling genuine scholarly influence from citation noise
ideas.description: This approach builds a heterogeneous citation network where each edge is weighted by the semantic function of the citation, determined through NLP classification of citation contexts into categories such as foundational, methodological, comparative, critical, or perfunctory. Graph centrality measures (e.g., weighted PageRank or eigenvector centrality) are then computed on this enriched network, ensuring that foundational and methodological citations contribute far more to a researcher's impact score than perfunctory or self-referential ones. Temporal decay functions and field-normalized edge weights are incorporated to prevent recency bias and cross-disciplinary distortion. The result is an impact metric that captures genuine intellectual influence rather than mere citation popularity, directly addressing the fundamental limitation that standard centrality approaches inherit the same biases as raw citation counts.

ideas.title: National Research Competitiveness Index via Composite Indicator Aggregation, Inspired by the Human Development Index
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from the UN's Human Development Index, which solved the problem of comparing national well-being by aggregating heterogeneous individual-level indicators (health, education, income) into a single composite national score using normalization and geometric averaging. We adapt this approach to bibliometrics: for each scientific domain, individual researcher h-indices within a country are aggregated using a two-component framework—a size component (sum or count-weighted total h-index reflecting absolute output) and an intensity component (per-capita or per-researcher mean h-index reflecting efficiency). Each component is min-max normalized across countries, and the geometric mean of the two yields a domain-level national score that balances research volume against quality density. Domain scores are then combined into an overall National Research Competitiveness Index (NRCI) using either equal or GDP-PPP-adjusted weights. This composite indicator methodology addresses key challenges in scaling h-index nationally: it avoids penalizing small countries with fewer researchers while still crediting large research ecosystems, it enables cross-domain comparison through normalization, and it produces a single policy-actionable number analogous to how HDI informs development policy. Validation can leverage known ground-truth rankings such as Nature Index or Leiden Ranking to assess construct validity.

ideas.title: Adapting epidemiological superspreader detection to identify high-impact researchers in citation networks
ideas.description: In computational epidemiology, identifying superspreaders in disease contact networks is a well-studied problem where simple degree centrality (number of contacts) fails to capture true transmission influence. Epidemiologists developed multi-scale approaches combining k-shell decomposition—which identifies nodes in the innermost core of a network—with transmission-weighted betweenness centrality to locate individuals whose structural position makes them disproportionately responsible for epidemic spread. This research adapts that framework to academic citation networks by treating the propagation of scientific ideas as analogous to disease contagion. Citations act as directed transmission edges, weighted by co-citation coupling strength to reflect intellectual proximity. Applying k-shell decomposition to the citation graph identifies researchers embedded in the densest knowledge core of a field, while transmission-weighted betweenness centrality reveals scholars who bridge otherwise disconnected research communities and serve as crucial conduits for cross-pollination of ideas. The resulting composite metric—a 'knowledge superspreader index'—would capture dimensions of scholarly influence invisible to h-index, raw citation counts, or even standard PageRank, particularly the ability to catalyze interdisciplinary diffusion. Validation could compare this index against expert panels' assessments of transformative researchers and track whether high-scoring scholars' work correlates with subsequent paradigm shifts in downstream fields.

ideas.title: Species Abundance Evenness Metrics Adapted for Citation Portfolio Analysis
ideas.description: In ecology, quantifying how evenly organisms distribute across species is a foundational problem solved through diversity indices such as Shannon entropy, Simpson's index, and the unifying Hill numbers framework. Hill numbers parameterized by order q allow researchers to tune sensitivity: q=0 measures pure richness, q=1 weights by abundance proportionally (equivalent to the exponential of Shannon entropy), and q=2 emphasizes dominant species (inverse Simpson). This elegant decomposition of diversity into richness and evenness components has proven far more informative than any single inequality metric. We propose transferring this ecological evenness framework to bibliometrics. Each researcher's publication portfolio is treated as a 'community' where papers are 'species' and citations are 'individuals.' Computing a citation diversity profile across Hill number orders generates a curve that reveals the full structure of citation inequality—not just a single summary statistic like the Gini coefficient. At q=0 we recover the total paper count, at q=1 we obtain a Shannon-based effective number of equally-cited papers, and at q=2 we capture how dominated the portfolio is by a few highly-cited works. Pielou's evenness (the ratio of observed to maximum Shannon diversity) provides a normalized 0-to-1 evenness score directly comparable across researchers with different publication counts. This multi-metric approach addresses known limitations of the Gini coefficient in bibliometrics, such as insensitivity to portfolio size and inability to distinguish between different shapes of inequality. The resulting citation diversity profiles can be used to classify researcher impact styles, track career evolution, and provide fairer cross-field comparisons of scholarly output distribution.

ideas.title: Adapting Customer Lifetime Value Prediction Models to Forecast Researcher Impact Trajectories
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy between predicting a researcher's future h-index and the well-established marketing problem of Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) prediction. In CRM analytics, models like BG/NBD and Pareto/NBD forecast a customer's total future value from early purchase behavior—frequency, recency, and monetary patterns—while accounting for population heterogeneity and the possibility of customer churn. We adapt this framework to scientometrics by mapping publications to transactions, citation counts to monetary value, and career departure to customer churn. A researcher's first five years of output provides analogous early-stage signals: publication frequency captures productivity, citation velocity captures impact momentum, and gaps between papers signal potential disengagement. The adapted model would use hierarchical Bayesian estimation (mirroring CLV's handling of customer heterogeneity) to learn population-level priors from large cohorts of researchers while making individualized predictions. This approach offers several advantages over standard regression or machine learning models: it provides principled uncertainty quantification, naturally handles researchers who leave academia (analogous to churned customers), and can incorporate field-specific base rates as informative priors. The model would be validated by training on researchers who began publishing before 2010 and evaluating predictions against their actual h-indices observed by 2025.

ideas.title: Sliding-window impact decomposition for career trajectory classification
ideas.description: This system tracks research careers using a rolling-window h-index computed over fixed 5-year publication windows, paired with contemporaneous publication rate, to decompose career trajectories into active impact versus passive citation accumulation. By discarding citations to papers outside the current window, the metric accurately distinguishes genuinely productive late-bloomers from researchers whose cumulative h-index rises only through legacy citations. A trajectory clustering algorithm then classifies careers into archetypes (early-peak, late-blooming, sustained, resurgent, dormant) based on the joint dynamics of windowed impact and output intensity. This addresses the fundamental flaw that the standard cumulative h-index is monotonically non-decreasing and therefore unable to detect productivity declines, career shifts, or true late-career breakthroughs.

ideas.title: Purchasing Power Parity for Academic Impact: Adapting Economic Currency Normalization to Cross-Discipline Citation Metrics
ideas.description: Cross-discipline comparison of researcher impact via h-index suffers from the same fundamental problem that comparing national economies via nominal GDP does: the underlying units (citations vs. currency) have vastly different 'exchange rates' across contexts. Economics solved this through Purchasing Power Parity (PPP), which constructs a standardized basket of goods for each country and computes a deflator that converts nominal values into comparable real values. We adapt this approach to bibliometrics by defining a standardized 'citation basket' for each academic field—comprising median citation counts for representative publication types (original research articles, reviews, conference proceedings) drawn from top-quartile venues over a rolling window. Each field's basket yields a citation deflator. A researcher's raw h-index is then divided by the deflator of their primary field, producing a PPP-adjusted h-index that reflects equivalent scholarly impact regardless of discipline-specific citation norms. Like economic PPP, the system accommodates temporal drift by recalibrating baskets periodically using comprehensive bibliometric data from sources such as OpenAlex or Scopus. This framework also naturally extends to researchers working across multiple fields by computing weighted composite deflators based on their publication distribution, analogous to how PPP handles multi-sector economies.

ideas.title: Ecosystem Services Valuation Framework for Research Impact
ideas.description: The h-index captures only one dimension of research value—academic citation—much like early ecosystem valuation captured only timber or fishery revenue. Environmental economics faced an analogous challenge: how to measure the full worth of natural capital when most value is invisible to market transactions. The Total Economic Value framework resolved this by systematically categorizing value streams (direct-use, indirect-use, option, and existence values) and deploying distinct measurement instruments for each. We adapt this approach to create a Total Research Impact (TRI) index. Direct-use impact corresponds to traditional citations and journal metrics. Indirect-use impact captures downstream effects such as patent references, clinical guideline inclusions, and software dependencies. Option impact measures potential future value through dataset downloads, preprint engagement, and replication attempts. Non-use impact reflects societal presence via media mentions, policy document citations, Wikipedia references, and public engagement metrics. Each category employs measurement instruments suited to its signal type—API-harvested altmetrics, patent database linkages, policy document text mining, and media tracking services. A normalizing and weighting scheme, calibrated per discipline using stakeholder surveys analogous to contingent valuation, aggregates these heterogeneous signals into a single TRI score. This framework preserves the simplicity of a single-number index while faithfully representing the pluralistic ways research creates value beyond academia.
